page_title: Sentinel Lymph Nodes (SLN) Biopsies: Clinical Aspects

items.item_title: What is a sentinel lymph node (SLN) and how is it identified?
items.text: A sentinel lymph node, or SLN, is defined as the specific lymph node to which a malignant tumour initially drains. The lymphatic system itself is present in most human tissues. Clinical recognition of the SLN is achieved by using an assortment of tracers, which can include radioisotopes and dyes. These tracers are infused into the peritumoral spots. As a consequence, the labelled SLN can be identified. It is then surgically removed so that histological cell analysis can be performed. This procedure is a standard practice for diagnosing and monitoring several types of cancer.

items.item_title: What is the role of SLN biopsy in breast cancer management?
items.text: In early-stage breast cancer, a disorder in the axillary lymph node is considered an initial signal. SLN biopsies are one of the safest procedures for these patients. They have low morbidity and a low rate of false negatives. Although one trial (NSABP B-04) found that adding axillary lymph nodes to a mastectomy did not affect general survival, the condition of the regional lymph nodes is still needed. This information is required for specific tailoring of adjuvant treatment and for assessing prognosis. Previously, if metastasis was diagnosed via SLN biopsy, a complete lymph nodal dissection (LND) was obligatory.

items.item_title: How has SLN biopsy changed the treatment for vulvar cancer?
items.text: Vulvar cancer is an unusual genital melanoma, with cases increasing in northern Europe since 1990\. Conventional treatments included radical vulvectomy and inguinal treatment. Inguinal lymph nodal dissection (LND) involves high-level morbidity. Patients could experience lymphedema, wound breakdown, and lymphocele formation. The GROINSS-V study showed that for patients with early-stage vulva cancer (tumour \<4 cm), the recurrence rate was analogous for both SLN biopsy and inguinal LND. Difficulties related to inguinal LND were also appreciably reduced. As a result, SLN biopsy is now a golden-standard method for women with tumours \<4 cm.

items.item_title: What is the rationale for SLN mapping and what improves its detection?
items.text: The useful feature of an SLN biopsy is the appropriate assortment of patients who will receive the advantages of a minimally insidious process. While surgical complications can occur, they are far less than those associated with LND. The integration of ultra-staging with the histological analysis of the SLN advances the detection rates. Conventional pathological methods were not able to find diseases in small volumes. Ultra-staging, however, can diagnose a single tumour in one million normal lymphocytes. Detection rates have also improved from the integration of new technologies like fluorescent dyes. As clinician research with dye methods progresses, the ability to detect will also increase.

page_title: Temperature Sensitive Mutants In Worms

items.item_title: What is a temperature-sensitive mutant in C. elegans?
items.text: A temperature-sensitive mutant is a powerful tool used in C. elegans biology. These are worms with a specific mutation, often engineered, that causes a protein to be functional at one "permissive" temperature but non-functional or inactivated at a different "sensitive" or "restrictive" temperature. This characteristic allows researchers to precisely control the activation or inactivation of a specific gene by simply shifting the temperature. This method is used to question the role of a protein, and its absence, during processes like development, cell division, or neuron formation.

items.item_title: How do C. elegans naturally sense temperature?
items.text: C. elegans possess a sophisticated system for sensing temperature, which is part of a process called thermotaxis. Several types of sensory neurons are involved. The AFD neurons are central to this process. They use a cGMP-dependent network of molecules to regulate an ion channel. Within this network, temperature itself regulates the concentration of cGMP in the cytoplasm. The level of cGMP is what determines how many cGMP-gated ion channels are open at a specific temperature. Other neurons, like the ASI chemosensory neurons, are also related to thermotaxis under very specific conditions, and their response is dependent on the AFD neurons.

items.item_title: How are temperature-sensitive mutants used in medical research?
items.text: C. elegans is used as a model organism to study several human neurodegenerative diseases, such as Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, ALS, and Huntington's diseases. In the case of ALS and Huntington's, it has been shown that temperature-sensitive mutant genes can act as modifiers for processes central to these diseases. Specifically, they can modify protein misfolding and the toxicity associated with protein aggregation. This allows researchers to use temperature shifts to control these pathogenic processes in a living animal model.

items.item_title: What are the main limitations of using temperature with C. elegans?
items.text:
While temperature control is useful, there are important constraints to consider.

 Sterility: If C. elegans* populations are grown at temperatures more than 25°C, the nematodes can become sterile. Since the worms are mostly hermaphrodites with the capacity to self-fertilize, this loss of reproductive capacity is a major issue. This makes accurate temperature control necessary when studying the worm's physiology.
* Male Production: The heat shock technique, while common for analyzing behavior, can also be a limitation. Too much exposure time to high temperatures (always over 25°C) can increase the production of males in the population. To avoid this bias, it is important to tightly control the timing of the temperature exposure.

page_title: From an organ on a chip to a body on a chip

items.item_title: What is a "body on a chip" system?
items.text: A "body on a chip" is a type of microphysiological system. It is created by interconnecting multiple, individual organ-on-a-chip (OoC) platforms into a single network. These single OoC systems integrate advanced 3D tissue-engineered constructs with microfluidic networks. This setup provides important physiological cues, such as vasculature and interstitial fluid flow, which helps emulate in vivo conditions. By connecting different organ systems, researchers can emulate the relationships between organs. This new in vitro tool has applications in personalized medicine, pre-clinical trials, toxicology, and drug development.

items.item_title: What is the main difficulty in combining different organs on one chip?
items.text: A primary difficulty is the heterogeneity of the culture conditions required for different organs. Up to now, organs-on-chips have been developed individually. Researchers have focused on finding the optimal conditions to maintain one single organ. These conditions include specific mechanical constraints, the three-dimensional architecture, and the exact composition of the medium. To build a functional body-on-chip, a common medium composition must be found that works for all the different organs. At the same time, this common environment must respect the specific and required environment that each individual organ needs to function correctly. This is a central issue to solve.

items.item_title: How must the vasculature be mimicked in a "body on a chip"?
items.text: The vasculature presents another difficulty for creating a functional "body on a chip". In the human body, organs are interconnected by blood vessels. These vessels are responsible for maintaining blood pressure, with assistance from the heart. To be as close as possible to human physiology, the microfluidic device must possess its own vasculature. This means that each microfluidic channel within the device has to mimic a blood vessel. In addition to the structure, the flow of the medium must also be considered. The medium flow will have to be "pulsatile" in nature. This is necessary to properly mimic the action of the heart pumping blood through the body.

items.item_title: What is the potential use of these systems in personalized medicine?
items.text: A "body-on-chip" system provides new features that are of high utility in the field of personalized medicine. These systems can be coupled with recent advances in cell culture, such as the use of induced pluripotent stem (IPS) cells. This specific combination would permit the culture of different organs all derived from one single patient. With such a patient-specific model, the efficiency of a particular pharmaceutical or a specific treatment procedure could be measured at the whole-body level. Furthermore, any side effects of the treatment could be easily spotted on each of the individual organs within the integrated system.

items.item_title: How can a "body on a chip" be used in toxicology?
items.text: These integrated systems are useful in the fields of toxicology and risk assessment. They can be used to easily identify the target organs for a specific chemical or a mixture of chemicals. The effects of the chemical can be characterized through medium collection and by direct organ observation. Additionally, the design of the chip can take into account various routes of exposure. This is important for the study of their differential effects on each organ. For example, an oral administration would contact the liver first, which would metabolize it. An intravenous administration would skip this step, distributing the chemical equally to all organs.

page_title: Blood brain barrier microfluidic models [Brain on Chip]

items.item_title: What is the purpose of a "brain on a chip" model?
items.text: Organs-on-chips are biological laboratories on a chip that are expected to permit robust and accurate drug testing\]. "Brain on chip" devices that mimic the blood-brain barrier (BBB) are a specific type of this technology\]. The BBB is a barrier of specialized endothelial cells that separates brain tissue from the blood system\]. Drugs must cross this barrier to target specific areas of the brain\]. Studying the BBB is therefore necessary to understand brain drug targeting\]. These models combine the advantages of in vivo and in vitro systems, providing a balance between mimicking reality and being a simple ex vivo model\].

items.item_title: Why are BBB-on-a-chip models needed as an alternative to animal and Petri dish studies?
items.text: In vivo animal techniques are still the best way to study the BBB's complex environment\]. However, 80% of drugs that are successfully tested on animals later fail in human clinical testing\]. This highlights the need for alternative solutions to animal testing, which is also costly, time-consuming, and ethically contentious\]. On the other hand, in vitro Petri dish solutions are easy models suitable for large-scale drug testing\]. Their drawback is that they do not reflect the complexity of the BBB and are not suitable for addressing complex scientific questions\]. BBB-on-a-chip models are being developed to overcome these respective limitations\].

items.item_title: How is the permeability of an on-chip BBB model characterized?
items.text: The first key parameter that must be characterized is the effective permeability of the barrier\]. Researchers must determine if the barrier is permeable and if this permeability is relevant compared to physiology\]. This is often done by tracking substances like FITC-labeled dextran or Evans-blue albumin\]. Standard Transwell systems are static and have a drawback, as solutes accumulate due to the absence of flow\]. Organs-on-chips offer an advantage because perfusion systems can be used\]. This allows for dynamic, real-time observation of permeability using live-microscopy techniques\].

items.item_title: What is Transendothelial Electrical Resistance (TEER) and how is it used in these models?
items.text: Transendothelial Electrical Resistance (TEER) is a parameter used to assess the tightness of the barrier\]. A tighter barrier, resulting from tight cell-cell junctions, will allow smaller amounts of charged molecules to be transported\]. This results in high electrical resistance\]. A 4-point metallic electrode measurement system is a key standard\]. It measures the system's conductivity, which is inversely proportional to resistance, to determine the tightness of the junctions\]. While this technique is non-invasive and label-free, it must be handled carefully in microfluidic devices, where high surface-to-volume ratios can affect the measurements\].

items.item_title: Why are shear stress and cell type important considerations for BBB chips?
items.text: To be powerful alternatives to animal testing, these models must use human cell models\]. While endothelial cells from brain capillaries have the right profile, this phenotype is lost in vitro over time\]. Human induced pluripotent stem cells (hiPSC) are of great interest for personalized medicine\]. In addition to endothelial cells, astrocytes and pericytes are also needed, as they are key to forming and maintaining the barrier's tight junctions\]. Shear stress is another important factor for endothelial differentiation and function\]. Unlike static models, microfluidics provides a way to mimic this shear stress by enabling highly controlled flow rates\].

page_title: Organotypic culture: from the beginning to 3D cell culture

items.item_title: What is organotypic culture?
items.text: Organotypic culture is a method defined by the cultivation of an organ that has been collected from an organism. It is a technique that permits the culture of intricate groups of cells or whole organs. A main characteristic of this method is the preservation of the original structure of the organ. Most of the cellular relationships within the organ are also maintained during the culture. Several different protocols have been created to perform this type of culture. Examples include methods using agar, watch glass protocols, and filter well inserts.

items.item_title: Why did the use of cell lines become more common than organotypic culture?
items.text: The culture of whole organs was reported at the end of the 19th century, before the first cell lines were established. The development of organotypic culture methods was slowed, however, once cell lines became available. The HeLa cell line, for instance, marked the start of intensive use of such systems by the scientific community. The widespread adoption of cell lines by researchers is related to the need for an experimental model with specific advantages. Scientists required a system that was dependable, consistent, inexpensive, and fast. These are all characteristics found in cell line cultures. This is reflected in the high number of articles and patents associated with lines like HeLa and MCF-7.

items.item_title: What are the drawbacks of using cell line models in research?
items.text: Despite the advantages of cell line models, certain drawbacks have appeared over time. One issue is the genetic deviation that can occur between two samples of the same cell line. This leads to a loss of consistency and dependability when these models are used. Another problem is the loss of certain cell functions. This happens because the culture conditions are designed to promote growth. They are not designed for the expression of a state that resembles a group of cells. An illustration of this is the difference in metabolism observed between a liver and a liver cell line. This distortion makes the application of cell line data to humans difficult or even unrealistic.

items.item_title: Why is there new interest in organotypic culture and three-dimensional models?
items.text: For a few years, pressure from regulatory agencies has led to a reconsideration of organotypic cultures. This is part of an effort to improve risk assessment for humans. While organotypic methods are more expensive, more intricate, and take more time, they offer better possibilities for applying data to humans. They are considered more applicable because they take the physiological environment into account. A need now exists for new systems that offer the advantages of both cell lines and organ culture. One of the solutions is the use of three-dimensional cell cultures, such as spheroids. The development of these newer techniques is based on the knowledge that was acquired from the two previous models.

page_title: Organs on chips and drug development

items.item_title: Why are 2D cell cultures considered insufficient for drug development?
items.text: 2D culture models are able to provide large amounts of data at a relatively low cost. This type of model, however, is considered to weakly represent the intricate pathophysiology found in patients. They often require computational modeling to predict in vivo drug responses. In contrast, biomimetic 3D tissue models are clinically more applicable. They are able to reproduce the involved interactions between different types of cells in vivo. These interactions are mediated by various soluble and insoluble substances, including cytokines, nutrients, and the extracellular matrix. These substances can all be managed within the organ-on-a-chip system.

items.item_title: What advantages do organs-on-chips offer over animal models?
items.text: Organs-on-chips also have benefits when compared to animal models. Animal surrogates for human diseases can emulate the real physiological condition of a whole organism. Despite this, they are now facing skepticism from scientists and reglementary agencies. This skepticism is about their translatability to humans. Microdevices have specific features that represent distinct advantages over both cellular and animal models. The microdevice is optically transparent, which allows for high-resolution analysis. They can have compartmentalized channel designs for multi-organ chips. They also provide the ability to manage microenvironment conditions. Media can also be sampled for the identification of novel biomarkers.

items.item_title: How are organ-on-a-chip models used for drug toxicity screening?
items.text: Unexpected adverse drug effects are a common cause of clinical trial failures. They are also responsible for the costly withdrawals of marketed drugs. Organ-on-a-chip models are being used to more reliably predict human toxicities. For example, these models have already demonstrated their ability to reduce the biological noise found in in vivo metabolomic models. Other chip models can replicate the contractile function of the heart muscle. This application can help avoid drug-induced cardiotoxicity. These models can, therefore, be used for the prediction of dose-limiting human toxicities. They also allow for the mechanistic investigation of these prospective drugs.

items.item_title: What is stratified medicine, and how can organs-on-chips be applied in this field?
items.text: Stratified medicine is the practice of developing drugs for particular patient populations. These populations are often stratified, or grouped, based on the presence of a particular biomarker that is linked to a disease or a drug response. This practice has become more prevalent in recent years. The organ-on-a-chip technology has been applied to this field. It is used to develop in vitro models that reflect the genetic underpinnings of variability in human drug responses. The combination of these chip platforms with iPS cells may provide a method that is relatively inexpensive. This method could generate patient- and population-specific model platforms. Such platforms could then be used to test drugs that are designed to target specific mutations or polymorphisms.

items.item_title: How can organ-on-a-chip models assist with research into rare diseases?
items.text: The development of new drugs for rare diseases is greatly hampered by two main issues. There is a lack of appropriate preclinical models to study the diseases. There is also a scarcity of patient populations available for clinical trials. Organs-on-chips are seen as a good solution to these problems. As an example, heart-on-a-chip models have been created using iPS cells. These models are used to study the weakness of cardiomyocytes in Barth syndrome. Another example is a model of hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia. This model uses a microfluidic device to engineer 3D vascular tissue, creating blood vessel-like tubular structures for study.

page_title: Drug screening: methods and application to oncology

items.item_title: What are the main stages of the drug discovery and approval process?
items.text:
The drug discovery pathway begins with the Drug R\&D phase. This phase includes Target ID & Validation (1-2 years), Hit Generation (1-2 years), Lead Generation & Optimization (1-2 years), and Pre-Clinical Animal Studies (1 year). After R\&D, the drug moves to Clinical Trials.

* Phase 1 assesses safety in 20-100 people (1-2 years).
* Phase 2 evaluates efficacy and safety in 100-300 people (1-2 years).
* Phase 3 confirms efficacy and safety in a larger group of 1,000-3,000 people (2-3 years). Finally, the process concludes with FDA Review & Approval, which typically takes 1-2 years.

items.item_title: What is the "oncogene addiction" concept in cancer therapy?
items.text: "Oncogene addiction" is a term that describes a tumor's heavy dependence on a single pathogenic oncoprotein for its growth and proliferation. This dependency is so central that when the abnormal oncoprotein is inhibited by a drug, the tumor cells die. This concept is important because it provides a highly specific target for drug development. A well-known example is melanoma with the BRAF V600E mutation. These tumors show a high response to vemurafenib, a drug that inhibits that specific oncoprotein, whereas tumors without the mutation respond poorly.

items.item_title: What is "synthetic lethality" and how is it used to target cancer?
items.text: Synthetic lethality is a paradigm for indirectly targeting a "tumor context" with drugs. It describes a situation where two DNA repair pathways are blocked simultaneously: one by a genetic loss already present in the tumor, and the other by a drug. This is illustrated by the development of PARP inhibitors. PARP is a family of enzymes that repairs DNA single-strand breaks. Researchers found that cells already possessing defects in a different DNA repair pathway were the most sensitive to PARP inhibitors. This genetic deficiency made the tumor more dependent on the PARP pathway. By using a drug to block this second pathway, the tumor's DNA repair is critically compromised, leading to enhanced cell killing.

items.item_title: How do 2D and 3D cell culture models differ for preclinical drug screening?
items.text:
2D and 3D cultures are both preclinical models used to test anticancer drugs, but they have different characteristics.

* 2D Cultures: These are tumor cell lines grown on a flat, 2-dimensional surface. Their advantages are that they are easy to manipulate, provide high reproducibility, and are good for high-throughput screening. However, they lack the realistic, heterogeneous, multi-cell-type microenvironment of a solid tumor and are unsuitable for studying processes like angiogenesis.
 3D Cultures: These systems, which include "organs-on-a-chip," allow tumor cells to grow in an anchorage-independent manner, similar to an in vivo* setting. They can include co-cultures with other cell types, such as stromal fibroblasts or endothelial cells, to create a more realistic microenvironment. This allows 3D models to be used for studying complex processes like metastasis, angiogenesis, and the role of immune cells.

items.item_title: What are the primary methods for discovering new drug molecules?
items.text:
Initial "hits" and "leads" for drug discovery can be identified through several methods, which are often used in sequence or in parallel.

* High-throughput Screening (HTS): This method evaluates large, diverse libraries of chemical structures (sometimes billions of compounds) in a specific assay to find initial hits.
* Fragment-Based Screening: This approach uses a library of relatively small compounds. It provides sensitive and precise information about how molecules bind to the target and can be a more effective way to find starting points for discovery.
* Structure-Based Design: This uses data from methods like crystallography, spectroscopy, and bioinformatics to inform drug design.
* Informatics: This involves using sophisticated algorithms for statistical and predictive modeling to analyze the massive amounts of data generated by modern robotic assays, micro-arrays, and parallel chemical syntheses.

items.item_title: What nonclinical studies are required to begin human testing for a new anticancer drug?
items.text:
The S9 guidance, published by the International Conference on Harmonisation, outlines the nonclinical studies needed to initiate human clinical trials for investigational anticancer agents. These studies are intended to support trials in cancer patients who have advanced disease and limited therapeutic options. The required studies include:

* Primary pharmacology
* Pharmacokinetics
* Safety pharmacology
* General toxicity

The goal is to speed the evaluation of new therapies without compromising patient safety, particularly ensuring no impact on a vital organ's function. Studies for reproductive toxicity, carcinogenicity, or genotoxicity are generally not needed to start the initial clinical investigations.

page_title: Spheroids as powerul 3D-cell culture intermediates

items.item_title: What is a spheroid, and why is it considered a useful 3D cell culture model?
items.text: A spheroid is a 3D, spherical organoid. It is used as a 3D model because cells within an organ are surrounded by other cells and physical constraints. This 3D environment is not replicated by classical 2D cell cultures. A need exists for a 3D model that is reproducible and inexpensive, like 2D cultures, but also provides a three-dimensional organisation. Spheroids are considered an intermediate system. They were first developed in the 1970s. Compared to standard monolayer cell cultures, spheroids show an increase in cell density. They also display greater biological functionality. This makes them a useful step toward replicating the organisation of a tissue or organ.

items.item_title: How do tumor spheroids compare to 2D monolayer cultures and in vivo tumors?
items.text: Spheroids and in vivo tumors share many characteristics that are absent in 2D monolayer cultures. For example, 2D cultures grow with spatial restriction, but spheroids and tumors do not. Both spheroids and in vivo tumors show a concentration gradient of O2, nutrients, and metabolic wastes. This leads to the formation of a hypoxic core and different biological zones, such as proliferative, quiescent, and necrotic areas. These features are not found in monolayers. Spheroids and tumors also possess a cancer stem cell niche. The glucose flux rate for spheroids and tumors is high, while it is low for monolayers. The gene expression profile of spheroids is reported as being similar to in vivo tumors, whereas the profile for monolayers is different.

items.item_title: What methods are used to cultivate tumor spheroids for research?
items.text: Tumor spheroids can be cultivated in different ways. They may contain only one single type of tumor cell or be more complex by containing multiple cell types. The diagrams show two methods for creating them. For a mono-culture, a single cell suspension of tumor cells is dispensed into matrix-coated wells. The cells then aggregate to form homogenous tumor spheroids. For a co-culture, the process is similar. A single cell suspension of tumor cells is combined with other cells, such as fibroblasts. This mixture is dispensed into the matrix-coated wells. These different cell types then aggregate together, forming heterogeneous multicellular tumor spheroids. These spheroid models are used as an intermediate system, positioned between 2D cell culture and tumor explant culture.

items.item_title: How are spheroids used in "tumor-on-a-chip" devices?
items.text: Spheroids are used within "tumors-on-a-chip," which are microengineered biomimetic models. These microfluidic devices are manufactured to contain a functional unit of a tumor. The device allows for the culture of either monocellular spheroids or co-cultures of tumor cells with other cell types. This is done within an architecture that is designed to replicate the tumor microenvironment. The microfluidic device integrates a chamber for the spheroid to attach. It also allows for the perfusion of culture medium. This medium can contain dissolved chemicals for testing. The chip, which is composed of an inert plastic polymer, may also integrate a porous membrane for advanced medium perfusion.

page_title: The Complexity Behind the 3D Cell Cultures: the 3D Liver Cell Culture Case

items.item_title: Why is a "liver on a chip" difficult to create?
items.text: Creating a "liver on a chip" is not simple because the liver's structure is elaborate. A complete solution has not yet been approached. This is because the organ contains different types of cells. These include hepatocytes, endothelial cells, Kupffer cells, and stellate cells. Although hepatocytes are the most numerous cell type, it would be an error to forget the other types. These cells interact with each other. Hepatocytes also require a very specific three-dimensional structure. This structure must be accomplished for them to be able to keep their liver functions. One difficulty is related to the specific architecture of the liver, as different cell types are found in different locations.

items.item_title: How are multicellular spheroids formed, and what is the associated risk?
items.text: Multicellular spheroid culture is a more recent process used for 3D cell cultures. The method is based on the property of self-assembly. Hepatocytes and other cell types aggregate on their own. This way of culture can be used for different applications. It only requires one parameter to be controlled, which is the size of the spheroid. A risk is associated with this size. If the spheroid becomes too big, the inner cells may not receive enough nutrients and oxygen. These inner cells can then enter a necrotic state. This condition could eventually kill all the cells in the aggregate. This is caused by the dissemination of necrotic messengers. It is therefore necessary to control and maintain the growth rate below this damaging point.

items.item_title: What are the advantages of using 3D liver cell cultures for toxicity tests?
items.text: One application for 3D liver cell cultures is in toxicity tests. These systems offer certain benefits over 2D cultures. They can be cultivated for a longer period. This is because they do not require trypsinization. Liver cell spheroids are noted for their stability. They also have a bigger surface area compared to monolayer cultures. This allows molecules to interact more easily with the cells. Additionally, the exposure to chemicals is different. Cells are exposed following a gradient of concentration. The outer cells are directly exposed. In contrast, the inner cells are exposed as the molecule diffuses inward. This is considered more physiologically relevant than a 2D monolayer. In a 2D system, all cells are equally exposed.

items.item_title: What are the difficulties associated with the miniaturisation of bioreactors for 3D liver culture?
items.text: Scaling down bioreactors for liver cell culture is a very difficult task. A principal consideration is the need to keep the necessary cell mass. This is the fixed minimum number of cells required to make the system reliable and functional. This mass must be considered if the size is reduced. A smaller environment also complicates the nutrient and oxygenation process. It becomes harder to reach all the cells as the space goes down. Additionally, the small volume of medium will be impoverished more quickly. Another point is that microdevices must be closed systems. This is necessary for them to compete with larger bioreactors. The feasibility of this feature at the micron scale must be considered.

page_title: How Far Are We From Embryogenesis in a Dish?

items.item_title: What was the primary technical limit for in vitro embryo culture, and why was it an issue?
items.text: Until recently, growing mouse or human embryos in vitro was technically restricted. Cultures were only able to reach the blastocyst stage of development. This stage corresponds to approximately five or six days. This limitation was a significant problem for research into human development. Many of the important events in embryogenesis, especially those related to developmental disorders, occur just after this period. The inability to sustain cultures beyond this point created a gap in knowledge. Therefore, a need to access and study embryos at older stages was noted by the scientific community. The inability to observe these later processes restricted the understanding of important organizational phases.

items.item_title: What recent technical improvement has been made in human embryo culture?
items.text: A new direction has been opened in human embryo culture due to recent findings. Technical developments made since 2016 have made it possible to culture human embryos for up to 13 days. This is an extension from the previous limit of the blastocyst stage. This extended culture period allows researchers to observe many of the structures and events that support the later growth of the embryo. Specifically, after 14 cell cycles, the embryo can be seen preparing for gastrulation. Gastrulation is described as the principal organizational event. It is responsible for creating the basic body plan and provides a base for building all tissues.

items.item_title: Why are embryonic stem cells used as a model, and what is the main challenge in using them?
items.text: Embryonic stem cells are considered a useful and easily handled experimental model. This is because of their ability to divide and be renewed. By definition, they also have the capacity to become any type of cell. This requires them to go through many stages of development. A central difficulty exists in using them for differentiation in vitro. While embryogenesis in vivo is well-organized in its spatial arrangement, the in vitro differentiation of these stem cells is not. This lack of organization is a problem. A development has been made in the possibility for researchers to engineer the microenvironment. It is now possible to supply devices and structures that make the environment suitable for three-dimensional tissue differentiation. These structures are designed to closely mimic the shape of a differentiating embryo.

items.item_title: What are the remaining challenges for mimicking embryogenesis, and what solutions are being explored?
items.text: One of the central drawbacks to this research is the limited availability of human embryos. Very few of these embryos are able to reach later developmental stages. This limits the source material for study. A possible solution is being explored to address this and to better mimic the natural environment. In the body, extra-embryonic tissues like the placenta or yolk sac furnish nutrients. They also send signals that direct the differentiation of pluripotent cells. This guidance occurs in a regulated spatial and temporal domain. To replicate this, researchers are combining embryonic stem cells with trophoblast stem cells. This combination has been tested in a gel that contains extracellular matrix molecules. The approach has produced encouraging results in mouse models, though these replicas were not perfect.

page_title: Liver on a chip state of the art

items.item_title: Why is it necessary to develop "liver on a chip" models?
items.text: The liver is the main organ involved in the metabolism of xenobiotics, which are foreign substances. It is therefore mandatory to test new chemicals on the liver during toxicological studies and drug development. A significant problem is that the models currently in use are badly predictive of the responses that are then observed in humans. Liver toxicity, known as Drug-Induced Liver Injury (DILI), is one of the major causes for the arrest of a new drug's development during the clinical phases. New models are needed to better assess hepatotoxicity and to more accurately predict and prevent the occurrence of DILI.

items.item_title: What kinds of high-throughput models have been created for liver studies?
items.text: The first model described as being close to a "liver on a chip" was an assembly of micro-pillars within microwells. This was a 532-well plate where each well had a 60nL capacity. In this system, hepatic cells were suspended in a Matrigel and subsequently placed onto the micro-pillars. This model is compatible with high-throughput screening (HTS) methods. It is also possible to use micropatterning techniques like soft lithography to create co-cultures of different cell types in a 384-well plate format. A main issue with some of these systems, however, remains the use of PDMS, which is a material that absorbs some drugs.

items.item_title: How are more complex, multi-cell type liver models being constructed?
items.text: More advanced liver models have been developed to include multiple cell types. One such liver model, from Pittsburg University, contains hepatocytes, endothelial cells, Kupfer cells, and stellate cells. The cells for this system are sourced from biopsies or non-usable organ donations, and the model is used for hepatotoxicity assessment. Another microfluidic device from BioMEMS aims to mimic hepatic sinusoids and contains the same cell types. Other approaches use iPS cells that are differentiated in a 3D scaffold, mimicking the environment of a hepatic lobule. This device can be coupled to other devices containing heart cells. A hepatic spheroid has also been bio-printed onto a microfluidic device.

items.item_title: What are the two main types of platforms used for "liver on a chip" systems?
items.text: Globally, two types of platforms are used for these models. The first type is a static platform. These systems are used without a perfusion system. The second type is a platform that includes a perfusion system. This latter type is used by researchers at MIT, who have developed a system with a perfusion system on a mono-layer of hepatic cells. This setup is used to evaluate the elimination of a pharmaceutical. This platform was later extended to be compatible with co-culture. Other researchers have also used perfusion systems. For example, Toh and colleagues have used one to generate a concentration gradient of a specific chemical.

items.item_title: What is a major limitation of current "liver on a chip" models?
items.text: A main issue with the "liver" organ-on-a-chip models that have been developed is related to a missing component. The bile ducts, which are part of the liver's digestive function, have not been recreated yet in these systems. Once this is successfully done, the next step would be to connect the "liver on a chip" with a "gut on a chip". Such a connection would provide a more global vision. This would help researchers understand the way a chemical is processed and washed out of the body. To advance beyond simple models, scientists are also developing physiology-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) approaches to model the reaction of the whole organism.

page_title: Heart On a Chip: State of the Art

items.item_title: Why are "heart on a chip" models being developed?
items.text: The heart is the central component of the cardiovascular system. If it becomes dysfunctional, other organs will not receive sufficient oxygen. A main reason for developing these models is that some pharmaceuticals cause cardiac rhythm impairment. This issue is a major cause of market withdrawal for new medicines, accounting for approximately one-third of cases. The models currently used, such as cell lines or animal testing, are not predictive enough of what is observed in humans. Furthermore, heart diseases are a significant public health concern and are responsible for 25% of the total number of deaths in the USA. New models are needed to address this problem.

items.item_title: How are induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells used in this research?
items.text: Induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells are used to create "heart on a chip" models that can mimic specific human diseases. A team at Berkeley University developed a chip from iPS cells that showed rhythmic contractile activity. More importantly, when iPS cells from a patient with a heart rhythm abnormality were used, the "heart on a chip" also displayed that same abnormality. This means the system can be very useful for mimicking diseases, which allows new treatments to be developed more efficiently. A collaboration involving the Wyss Institute uses iPS cells from both healthy and unhealthy patients. This work aims to create personalized models for testing pharmaceuticals in a personalized medicine context.

items.item_title: What methods have been used to replicate the heart's mechanical functions on a chip?
items.text: Different approaches have been used to replicate the mechanical actions of the heart. The heart's beat is generated by the organ itself, so it will continue to beat without innervation. One team cultivated rat cardiomyocytes on stripes, which caused the cells to align and start beating. This system allowed for the measurement of beat rate, electrophysiology, and contractility after chemical exposure. In another study, a cylindrical bioreactor was built to cultivate embryonic heart cells. These cells were exposed to a pulsated flow that was designed to mimic the heart cycle in the left ventricle. With video and pressure sensors, this system allowed the beat (diastole and systole) to be characterized and for high or low blood pressure to be simulated.

items.item_title: What is the future direction for "heart on a chip" technology?
items.text: The future of "organ on a chip" research related to the heart is focused on the interconnection of several organs. This approach is intended to better predict the effect of a given chemical at the scale of the entire organism. Heart disease is a serious global health problem, and the number of deaths from heart failure is rising. Most available treatments only slow the progression of the disorder. This situation creates a great need for the development of new therapies. These new solutions require more insight into the molecular and genetic nature of the diseases. It is believed that this insight cannot be obtained without human heart disease models, such as those provided by "Organ on Chip" technology.

page_title: Pancreas on a chip : State of the Art

items.item_title: What are the main functions of the pancreas and why are "pancreas on a chip" models needed?
items.text: The pancreas is a gland attached to the digestive system. It has two main roles: one endocrine and one exocrine. The exocrine function involves secreting digestive fluids that contain enzymes. These enzymes help with the digestion of fatty acids. The endocrine function is based on secreting hormones, like insulin, that regulate glycemia. Pancreatic cancer is generally associated with a bad prognosis, so new therapies are needed. "Pancreas on a chip" models are being developed to study the organ's functions and diseases like diabetes.

items.item_title: What problem with type 1 diabetes treatment are these chip models trying to address?
items.text: Type 1 diabetes is identified as an auto-immune disease. This condition is marked by the destruction of the Langherans $\\beta$ islets. One treatment, aside from symptomatic ones, is islet transplantation. These transplantation treatments often fail. This failure is due to several factors. Cellular damage can be linked to the process of isolating the islets. Hypoxia is another reason for failure. "Pancreas on a chip" models are used to investigate these problems. For example, they can monitor islet function to help select only healthy islets for transplantation. Devices are also used to study the mechanisms of transplantation failure itself, such as the effects of hypoxia or interactions with macrophages.

items.item_title: How have microfluidic chips been used to assess the health of individual pancreatic islets?
items.text: A PDMS chip was created by a team at Michigan University. This device allows for the assessment of 15 individual mice islets. The assessment is based on their secretion of insulin. The authors of that study consider using this device to investigate the basic origins of type 1 diabetes. A further use is to improve the success rate of islet transplantation. This improvement would be achieved by monitoring the function of the islets before the procedure. In this way, only the healthy islets would be selected for the transplantation. Another platform, developed at Chicago University, also allows for the culture of individual islets encapsulated in a hydrogel for real-time imaging. This chip is specifically designed to trap single rat or human islets.

items.item_title: In what ways have co-culture models been used to study the pancreas on a chip?
items.text: Co-culture models have been used to study cellular interactions and improve islet survivability. A team in Shandong University developed a chip for the co-culture of bone marrow and pancreatic cells. This device allows for the study of dynamic processes related to stem cell migration from the bone marrow to the islet. A Korean team developed a chip with microwells for the co-culture of islets and fat tissue spheroids from rats. It was found that this co-culture increases the survivability of the islets. This finding suggests a potential increase in transplantation success. A team in Seoul also built a chip with interconnections between pancreatic islets and gut cells. Additionally, the TissUse Company evidenced communication between liver and pancreas models on a chip.

items.item_title: What are the main limitations of current "pancreas on a chip" technology?
items.text: Despite many improvements, current models have limitations. It is not possible to recreate both the endocrine and exocrine functions of the pancreas within a single device. The pancreas has two main roles. The exocrine function is the secretion of digestive fluids. The endocrine function is the secretion of hormones to regulate glycemia. Current models generally focus on one of these roles, but not both. Furthermore, another specific part of the organ has not yet been built on a chip. The ductal compartment of the pancreas has never been successfully recreated in one of these models. These challenges show that further development is still needed in this area.

page_title: Skin on a chip state of the art

items.item_title: What is the purpose of developing "skin on a chip" models?
items.text: The skin is a complex system, acting as the body's outer envelope with multiple layers, appendages, and sensors. It also has an inherent temperature gradient. "Skin on a chip" models are being developed to improve how skin toxicity is tested. Current models are often based on animals. These animal models can be unpredictable. This is because the physiology and immunity at the level of the skin are different from humans. Due to 3R regulations, synthetic skin models have emerged. The objective for these synthetic models is to be more predictive for skin toxicity than the animal models.

items.item_title: What environmental conditions are considered necessary for in vitro skin cultures?
items.text: Specific conditions are needed for the proper functionalization of in vitro skin cultures and models. The skin's role as an outer barrier makes these requirements important. A main requirement is the presence of an air/liquid interface. This arrangement mimics the skin's natural exposure to the air on one side and contact with the body's internal environment on the other. Additionally, a temperature gradient is considered necessary for good functionalization of the cultures. The skin in the body naturally has a temperature gradient, which ranges from 37°C internally down to room temperature at the surface. Replicating these two conditions is a focus for in vitro models.

items.item_title: How are induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells used in "skin on a chip" systems?
items.text: Induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells have been used to create 3D skin models. One team developed a "skin on a chip" in a culture insert using iPS cells. These cells were grown in 3D. They showed the ability to recreate the different layers of the skin in a way that was comparable to human skin. A benefit of this approach is the ability to use iPS cells derived from a patient with a skin disease. This allows for the testing of new treatments or the cutaneous toxicity of molecules in a pathological state. This was not possible with previous in vitro models. Endothelial iPS cells have also been used, which adds vascularization that strengthens the basal lamina.

items.item_title: How have researchers combined different cell types to create more complex skin models?
items.text: Several models have been established by co-culturing different cell types. One team from Singapore created a model with both keratinocyte (HaCaT KC) and monocyte (U937) cell lines. This system permitted the monitoring of transepithelial electric resistance (TEER) and allowed immunological tests. Another Korean team reconstituted all three layers of the skin (hypodermis, dermis, and epidermis) using different cell lines (HaCaT KC, HS27 FB CL, HUVEC). These layers were cultured on three PDMS supports and separated by porous membranes. This model was able to reproduce edema and inflammation. An Italian team created a fully innervated "skin on a chip" by combining human dermal fibroblasts, a keratinocyte cell line, and neurons from DRG rats.

page_title: Muscle on a Chip: State of the Art

items.item_title: Why is developing a "muscle on a chip" considered necessary?
items.text: Skeletal muscles are a large part of human body weight, accounting for 40%, and they use about 30% of energy intake. A large loss of muscle mass often accompanies chronic pathologies, which can worsen a patient's general condition. Curative treatments are not yet available for most musculoskeletal disorders. For example, diseases such as Duchenne’s disease and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis still lack effective treatment. Also, some treatments for chronic conditions like diabetes have undesired effects on the striated muscles. Access to a "muscle on a chip" would permit study into these adverse effects. It would also allow for a better prediction of a drug candidate's effects during the clinical phase.

items.item_title: What are the basic characteristics of a striated muscle cell?
items.text: The striated muscle cell is also known as a rhabdomyocyte. This type of cell is multinucleated. It is also described as being rich in mitochondria. The proteins responsible for contraction are myosin and actin. This contraction process is dependent on an inflow of intracellular calcium. These are the basic cellular properties. Understanding them is necessary for building functional muscle models. These models are used in research. For instance, recreating the calcium-dependent contraction is a central validation step for a "muscle on a chip".

items.item_title: How has a "muscle on a chip" been shown to mimic physiological muscle function?
items.text: A muscle unit was recreated by one team from Duke University. This was done by culturing human muscle cells obtained from volunteers. An electrical stimulus was applied to this "muscle on a chip". It was shown that the muscle contracts in proportion to the intensity of the current that was applied. This demonstrates a controlled response. It was also found that the contraction passes through the release of calcium. This mechanism is exactly the same as what is observed in physiological conditions. Another team from MIT created a neuro-muscular unit on a chip. In this model, engineered neurons respond to light stimuli. The stimulated neurons then excite the muscle cells, causing them to contract. This shows a functional connection between nerve and muscle cells in the chip model.

items.item_title: How have "muscle on a chip" models been used to study specific diseases?
items.text: Models of "muscle on a chip" have been applied to study Duchenne disease. One group from Brown Medical School Hospital reconstructed a model using myoblasts from MDX mice. These mice are genetically modified to have Duchenne myodystrophy. The bio-artificial muscles were grown in 3D within a gel that reproduced the extracellular matrix. This system was developed in a PDMS chip. Another original approach was developed by researchers from the Wyss Institute. They applied a "muscle on a chip" to the tongue. This model was also used to study the mechanisms of Duchenne's disease. It was shown by the authors that muscle fibres from patients with this disease were smaller. They also had a different polarization compared to healthy rhabdomyocytes, which results in fewer contraction capabilities.

items.item_title: What is considered the next step in developing "muscle on a chip" technology?
items.text: The future for "organs on a chip" that are dedicated to muscle is described as concerning the connection to other organs. This would be accomplished by the creation of vessels that have a contraction capacity. The objective of this work is to have the most complete vision possible of how a molecule is distributed in the human body. This integration would allow for more complex studies. Such studies would better replicate the systemic interactions that occur within a living organism. This would represent a move from isolated muscle models to interconnected organ systems.

page_title: Super Resolution Microscopy: The Diffraction Limit of Light

items.item_title: What is the diffraction limit in optical microscopy?
items.text: The diffraction limit of light is a critical limit in optical resolution. It is caused by the diffraction of visible light wavefronts as they pass by a circular aperture. This aperture is located at the rear focal plane of an objective lens. This phenomenon is considered the most relevant factor affecting the final resolution of an optical imaging system. A microscope is not able to resolve two objects if they are located at a distance shorter than $\\lambda/2NA$. The variable $\\lambda$ represents the wavelength of light, while $NA$ is the numerical aperture of the imaging lens. This means that the diffraction limit does not permit the microscope to differentiate between two objects when they are divided by a lateral distance. This distance must be less than half of the wavelength of the light used for imaging.

items.item_title: What did Ernst Abbe discover about microscope resolution?
items.text: The limitations of microscopes caused by the diffraction of light were discovered by a German physicist named Ernst Abbe in 1873\. His research revealed that the resolution of a microscope is not related to the quality of the microscopy components themselves. It was found to be due to the wavelength of the light being used and the aperture of the optics. The resolution is understood to be inversely proportional to the wavelength of the light that is observed. It is also directly proportional to the size of the objective itself. It was also discovered by Abbe that images are made of an array of diffraction-limited spots. These spots have modifiable intensities. They overlap one to another to produce the final image.

items.item_title: What are the advantages of using Super-Resolution Structured Illumination Microscopy (SR-SIM)?
items.text: Super-Resolution Structured Illumination Microscopy (SR-SIM) increases the spatial resolution over wide-field microscopy by a factor of two. Both lateral and axial resolutions are improved, by 100nm and 250nm respectively. The technique also permits 4D images to be obtained. This can be done at a fast frame rate. This speed is possible because only nine to fifteen raw images are normally necessary for 2D or 3D-SIM reconstruction. Other techniques, such as STORM or PALM, require at least one thousand images. Another benefit is that the use of conventional fluorophores is possible. This gives researchers access to existing biological mutated cell lines. Up to four different wavelengths can also be used, making the technique useful for co-localization. Finally, the preparation of samples for SR-SIM observation is described as easier than for other super-resolution techniques.

items.item_title: What are the limitations associated with the SR-SIM technique?
items.text: Several limitations are associated with the SR-SIM technique. The method is described as very sensitive to out-of-focus light. This sensitivity makes it very hard to focus when samples are thick. It is also difficult to focus on samples that are densely labelled. A further limitation is related to the achievable resolution. While techniques like PALM, STORM, or STED can reach a 20nm lateral diffraction limit, SR-SIM is limited to 200nm. Finally, issues can arise during the image reconstruction step. It is noted that some artefacts may be generated during this process. These potential artefacts can damage the quality of the final image.

page_title: Super-Resolution Structured Illumination Microscopy (SR-SIM)

items.item_title: What is the main principle of SR-SIM?
items.text: The principle of Super-Resolution Structured Illumination Microscopy (SR-SIM) involves using light patterns that are generated by interference. These patterns are used to create a Moiré effect, which allows information with higher resolution to be extracted. A Moiré effect is the superposition of two periodic structures, such as two high spatial frequency patterns, that produces a low spatial frequency pattern when they overlap. In an SR-SIM setup, laser beams interfere at the focal plane of the objective. This interference creates an illumination pattern in stripes. The high frequency of these stripes combines with the high-frequency organization of the objects in the sample, creating a low-frequency pattern that can be resolved.

items.item_title: How is a super-resolved image reconstructed in SR-SIM?
items.text: To reconstruct the final super-resolved image, several raw images must be collected. It is necessary for each of these images to be acquired using different orientations of the structured illumination. This reorientation is accomplished by moving the diffraction grating, which involves both translation and rotation. The number of raw images needed depends on the desired dimensions. For example, to obtain a 2D-SR-SIM image, nine raw images are necessary. This set of images is acquired using three translations and three rotations of the grating. To get a 3D image, more images are required. A 3D reconstruction uses fifteen images, which are obtained by using five translations and three rotations.

items.item_title: What are the main benefits of using the SR-SIM technique?
items.text: SR-SIM increases the spatial resolution by two times over standard wide-field microscopy. The lateral resolution is improved by 100nm, and the axial resolution is improved by 250nm. Another benefit is the ability to obtain 4D images at a fast frame rate. This is possible because only nine to fifteen raw images are needed for reconstruction, which is much fewer than the thousand or more images required by STORM or PALM techniques. Furthermore, conventional fluorophores can be used, which gives access to existing biological mutated cell lines. Up to four different wavelengths can be used, making the technique useful for co-localization studies. Finally, the sample preparation for SR-SIM observations is noted as being easier than for other super-resolution methods.

items.item_title: What limitations are associated with SR-SIM?
items.text: This super-resolution microscopy technique has some limitations. It is very sensitive to light that is out-of-focus. It is also very hard to focus when samples are thick or when they are densely labelled. Additionally, a limit on resolution is noted. While PALM, STORM, or STED techniques are able to reach a 20nm lateral diffraction limit, structured-illumination microscopy's limit is 200nm. Finally, artefacts may be generated during the image reconstruction process. These artefacts can damage the overall quality of the final image. These factors must be considered when selecting a method.

page_title: Imaging of mitochondria positioning on microtubule using fast temperature regulation

items.item_title: Why is mitochondrial movement important and how is it regulated?
items.text: The proper movement and distribution of mitochondria are considered critical. This importance is due to their localized function within the cell. These functions are necessary for cell metabolism, growth, and survival. In mammalian cells, the mechanisms for positioning mitochondria appear to be dependent on the microtubule cytoskeleton. This network is used to distribute the mitochondria. The mitochondria are carried as cargos by specific motors. These motors are identified as kinesin or dynein. They transport the mitochondria along the microtubule network, ensuring they are distributed correctly to support their localized functions.

items.item_title: What are the different timescales involved in mitochondria positioning?
items.text: An interesting point is raised regarding the timescales of the components involved. The dynamics of the microtubules themselves occur very quickly. This timescale is reported to be in seconds. In contrast, the distribution of the mitochondria, which uses the microtubules, happens much more slowly. The timescale for mitochondria distribution is reported to be in minutes. This difference presents a challenge. A question is posed about how the cell manages to couple these two different time constants. How the rapid, seconds-long dynamics of the microtubules are coupled to the slower, minutes-long process of distributing mitochondria is a key question.

page_title: A drug-compatible and temperature controlled microfluidic device for live-cell imaging

items.item_title: What is a stated problem with using the polymer polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) for microfluidic systems in cell biology?
items.text: A problem with many microdevices is their reliance on the polymer polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS). This material is noted to absorb many types of molecules used in cell biology. This absorption from the microsystems creates a difficulty. The capacity to correctly modulate the medium's composition is affected. It also affects the ability to control the concentration of specific compounds inside the microchips. This interference has consequences for the dependability of these experiments. The observation of cellular responses to changes in growth conditions is part of studying cellular functions. The manipulation of cells and their environment during observation remains a significant difficulty.

items.item_title: What was developed to address the absorption problem of PDMS in microdevices?
items.text: New microdevices for cell observation were developed to address the described problem. These new systems are PDMS-free. This design means they do not interact with small molecules. The problem with PDMS-based systems is that they are known to absorb compounds used in cell biology. This absorption reduces the ability to correctly change the medium's composition. It also affects controlling the concentration of substances inside the microchips. The dependability of experiments is therefore affected. The new PDMS-free systems were created to solve this difficulty. They are intended for use during the observation of living cells. These systems provide a different way to manage the cellular surroundings.

items.item_title: What capabilities for temperature control are included in the new PDMS-free devices?
items.text: The new devices, which are made without PDMS, include a specific function for temperature management. This function is designed to maintain a set sample temperature. This control can be set both above and below the surrounding ambient temperature. In addition to maintaining a set temperature, the function allows for quick temperature shifts. This capability is part of the new design. These systems were created to allow for the manipulation of cells and their surroundings during microscopic observation. The ability to make fast changes to temperature is a part of this. The devices are meant to be used for observing living cells. The temperature control is combined with the PDMS-free construction, which does not interact with small molecules.

items.item_title: How are changes to the cellular environment managed during microscopic observation with these devices?
items.text: The new PDMS-free systems are designed to allow changes in medium composition. They also permit changes in temperature. These alterations can be effectively made inside the microchips. This is done while cell activity is under observation by microscopy. The manipulation of cells and their surroundings during observation experiments is a known difficulty. These systems can also be used with different model systems. They are presented as a flexible option for managing the cellular environment. The design avoids the absorption of small molecules, which is a problem with PDMS. This allows for more dependable modulation of medium contents and compound concentrations.

page_title: Yeast: Spindle And Microtubule Assembly

items.item_title: Why is S. pombe used for microtubule studies?
items.text: Fission yeast (S. pombe) is often used for investigations into microtubule assembly. The organism is considered simple to manipulate in a laboratory setting. S. pombe also has a short dividing time. The availability of temperature-sensitive mutant strains is another reason for its use. These specific mutants permit the examination of microtubule function in different areas of the yeast's biology. Temperature itself is a factor that controls the assembly of microtubules. These characteristics make the organism suitable for genetic and cellular studies of the cytoskeleton. Microtubules are involved in cell division, growth, and polarity, all of which have been well documented in this model system.

items.item_title: What is the basic structure of a microtubule?
items.text: A microtubule is described as a hollow cylinder. It is a polarized, non-covalent polymer that possesses a plus and a minus end. The structure is composed of 13 protofilaments. Each protofilament is itself composed of heterodimers of alfa and beta-tubulin. The assembly of these structures occurs in three phases. The first phase is nucleation, which involves the formation of the alfa and beta-tubulin dimers. In S. pombe, this nucleation happens at a perinuclear centre called the spindle pole body. The second phase is elongation. During this growing phase, tubulin dimers are added to the plus end of the microtubule. This addition allows the microtubule to extend away from its organizing centre. The final phase is equilibration, which is reached when the concentration of polymerized tubulin equals the concentration of free tubulin in the cytoplasm.

items.item_title: How does temperature affect microtubule stability?
items.text: The stability of microtubules is dependent on temperature once they are formed. Exposing cells to a low temperature, such as 6°C, is sufficient to cause microtubule depolymerization. This is sometimes referred to as a cold-shock. This process has been shown to be reversible. Microtubules are able to polymerize again when heat is reapplied. In experiments with yeast cells expressing GFP-Atb2 tubulin, this effect was observed. At 22°C, the microtubules showed a typical elongated shape. A complete depolymerization was seen when the cells were at 6°C. This was characterized by a loss of fluorescence from the microtubules and an increase in cytoplasmic fluorescence, suggesting tubulin dimers were released. When the temperature was returned to 22°C, the microtubule network re-polymerized.

items.item_title: How are temperature-sensitive mutants used to study spindle formation?
items.text: The induction of a changed state in temperature-sensitive mutants can be studied using temperature shifts. An experiment was performed using cut7-24ts yeast cells that also expressed GFP-Atb2. The Cut7 protein is a kinesin that is involved in the formation of the mitotic spindle. At the permissive temperature of 22°C, a correct bipolar spindle was formed and visible. The temperature was then shifted to 35°C, which is the restrictive temperature for this mutant. After a few minutes at this temperature, the bipolar spindle retracted. It adopted a monopolar shape, which was marked by a shortening in the spindle's length. This change was reversible. When the temperature was shifted back to the permissive level, the original spindle shape and length were retrieved after a few minutes.

page_title: Tracking viral entry

items.item_title: How do viral particles generally begin infection and enter a host cell?
items.text: Viral infection is initiated by the attachment of viral particles to the host cell membrane. This attachment is an intricate process. During this step, viral capsid proteins bind to specific receptors located on the cell membrane. These receptors vary in composition; they can be proteins, carbohydrates, or lipids. After attachment is complete, the viral particle is then internalised by the cell. One of two main methods is used for this internalisation. The first is endocytosis, where the cell takes in the particle. The second is membrane fusion, where the virus merges with the host cell's membrane. The specific entry method that is used is dependent on the type of virus. In particular, the method depends on whether the virus is an enveloped or a non-enveloped virus.

items.item_title: What are the different ways enveloped and non-enveloped viruses enter cells?
items.text: The method of entry is different for non-enveloped and enveloped viruses. Non-enveloped viruses, such as influenza, typically enter the cell through endocytosis. The poliovirus, which is a non-enveloped virus, can use two different strategies. It can be endocytosed, after which a viral protein forms a pore in the vesicle membrane to allow the viral RNA to penetrate. Alternatively, the poliovirus can attach to the cell membrane and release its RNA material directly into the cell. Enveloped viruses, like HIV or Ebola virus, use membrane fusion to enter cells. First, the viruses dock to the host cell. Specific viral proteins bind to membrane receptors, which bridges the two membranes. Following this, the outer layer membranes of the virus and the host cell fuse together. The capsid or viral genome is then released into the host's cytoplasm.

items.item_title: How is viral entry studied, and what labelling methods are used for observation?
items.text: The entry of viruses into cells can be studied by using fluorescent labels on different parts of the virus. The viral membrane, the viral content, or the viral core can be labelled. Observations of single viral particles as they enter and move inside the host cell are made possible with microscopy of living cells. The visualisation of viral particles is accomplished through the use of fluorescent proteins or different types of dyes. Examples of these dyes include amino-reactive dyes or lipophilic dyes. A consideration is that the labelling process itself can affect the infectivity of the virus. The correct amount of dye must be determined for each specific study. The chosen labelling strategy also affects what can be observed. For instance, if the viral membrane is labelled, fusion events will be seen as a disappearance of the fluorescence. If the viral content is also labelled, the transport of the viral particles inside the cell can then be followed after fusion.

items.item_title: How is temperature control used to investigate the mechanisms of viral entry?
items.text: The process of a virus entering a cell is known to be dependent on temperature. This dependency can be used in experiments to determine the method of entry. For example, a study in 2007 on the poliovirus was conducted using observation of living cells. A series of experiments were performed at 37°C and 12°C. It was determined by the researchers that poliovirus could no longer infect the cell at 12°C. This finding revealed that the virus was using an endocytosis mechanism to get inside the cell. Experiments of this type require a specific microscope setting. The setting must include a thermally controlled environment to maintain cell temperature during manipulations. The ability to rapidly switch the temperature is noted as a way to control both the entry of the virus and its subsequent transport inside the cells. For instance, infection can be started by switching from 4°C to 37°C, or paused by switching to 12°C.

page_title: Yeast: Cell Cycle And Thermosensitive Mutations

items.item_title: What is Schizosaccharomyces pombe?
items.text: Schizosaccharomyces pombe is a type of fission yeast. It was first isolated from millet beer in Africa in 1893\. This organism is a unicellular eukaryote. It is identified by its characteristic rod shape. The diameter of the yeast cell is around 3 micrometres. Its length is variable and depends on its status in the cell cycle. A cell can measure between 8 and 13 micrometres long. S. pombe is recognized for having a short generation time, which is two to four hours. This, combined with its direct genetics, made it suitable for foundational research. It was used in important studies concerning cell cycle regulation. The organism is used to study cell division, cell polarity, and microtubule assembly.

items.item_title: Why is S. pombe used in genetic research?
items.text: S. pombe is considered a useful organism for genetic research. It has a short generation time, lasting only two to four hours. Its genetics are also considered simple and direct to work with. These features allow experiments to be conducted quickly. The organism has been used for important foundational studies on the regulation of the cell cycle. Researchers use this yeast to examine many different cellular processes. These studies include cell division, the assembly of microtubules, and cell polarity. It is also applied in phenotype-genotype analyses. The speed of conducting experiments in fission yeast is noted as a benefit for research.

items.item_title: What is a thermosensitive mutant in S. pombe?
items.text: A thermosensitive, or ts, mutant is an organism used in research. An example in S. pombe is the Cdc25 ts mutant. In these mutants, a specific protein's function is dependent on the temperature. The protein is functional at one temperature, which is called the "permissive" temperature. For the Cdc25 mutant, the permissive temperature is 25°C. At this temperature, the protein works as normal. However, the protein becomes non-functional, or inactivated, when moved to a different temperature. This is known as the "restrictive" temperature. For Cdc25, this inactivation occurs at temperatures over 37°C. This property allows researchers to control the protein's function by changing the temperature.

items.item_title: How are Cdc25 thermosensitive mutants used to study the cell cycle?
items.text: The Cdc25 protein is required for the cell to enter mitosis. Researchers use Cdc25 thermosensitive (ts) mutants to control this step. When the yeast cells are moved to the restrictive temperature (over 37°C), the Cdc25 protein is inactivated. This inactivation causes a cell cycle arrest. The arrest specifically happens at the G2/M transition. This effect is reversible. If the cells are shifted back down to the permissive temperature (25°C), the protein becomes functional again. This allows the cells to re-enter the cell cycle. A main benefit of this method is that it creates a synchronous re-entry. All the cells in the culture can be made to enter the cell cycle at the same time, which is useful for study.

page_title: Human Cells: Microtubules Experiments

items.item_title: How does temperature affect microtubules in mammalian cells?
items.text: Microtubules in mammalian cells are known to be regulated by temperature. This process has been shown to be reversible. For example, in experiments using HeLa cells that express GFP-Atb2 (tubulin), a spider-like network of microtubules is observed at 37°C. This network elongates from the microtubule organising centre. When these cells are subjected to a temperature of 5°C for six minutes, complete depolymerization of the microtubules is induced. This change is seen as a decrease in fluorescence associated with the microtubules. An increase in fluorescence in the cytoplasm is also seen, which indicates the tubulin dimers have depolymerized into the free cytoplasmic pool. When the temperature is returned to 37°C, the microtubule network re-polymerizes.

items.item_title: What other cellular processes are regulated by temperature?
items.text: Besides microtubule assembly, many processes at the cellular level are regulated by temperature. It has been noted that endocytosis is arrested at 19°C. At temperatures lower than this, vesicular transport is blocked. The aggregation of proteins can also be induced by exposure to either low or high temperatures. This effect can also be reversed. Other areas of study that involve temperature control include endocytosis arrest and the complete stop of vesicular transport and trafficking. Temperature-gated ion channels can be induced. Protein recombination can be started and then tracked. The first steps of stem cell differentiation can also be recorded. Additionally, virus entry and its subsequent trafficking inside cells can be followed with microscope imaging.

items.item_title: How was a temperature-shift experiment on microtubules performed?
items.text: An experiment was conducted using HeLa cells that express GFP-Atb2, which is a tubulin marker. These cells were plated on glass coverslides. Biocompatible tape was added to the coverslides to delimit the cultured area. The cells were cultured in DMEM with 10% FBS at 37°C and 5% CO2. On the day of the experiment, the coverslides were harvested. A drop of medium was added to the cells. A specific temperature control chip and coverslip system was then placed onto the cells. The experiment was performed using an inverted Nikon TE2000e microscope. A 100/1.45 oil immersion objective was used for observation. Images were acquired using Metamorph 7.5 software to control the microscope and a Hamamatsu CCD camera.

items.item_title: How does the described temperature control device avoid shear stress on the sample?
items.text: The described temperature controller device uses microfluidic technology. It is designed to prevent fluid flow shear stress from affecting the sample. The system is based on a PDMS chip. This chip contains microchannels, which permit the circulation of a thermalized fluid. The chip is crosslinked to a coverslip. It is also connected to Peltier devices that are set at two different hot and cold temperatures. This setup makes it possible to quickly change from low to high temperatures.
