An electronic controller manages the Peltier elements. In this specific system, the thermalized fluid circulates within the chip's microchannels. The fluid is never in direct contact with the biological sample. This separation is what prevents any shear stress from being applied to the cells during the temperature change.

page_title: Ion channels experiment

items.item_title: What is the function of temperature-gated ion channels?
items.text: Sensing hot and cold temperatures is necessary for all organisms, allowing them to adapt to their environment. The cellular pathways that ensure thermo-sensation are conserved through evolution. These pathways consist of a set of specialised ion channels. An ion channel opens in response to a specific signal, which permits ions to go inside or outside of the cell. The signal for opening can be electric, mechanic, or molecular. For temperature-gated ion channels, the signal is either heat or cold. In mammals, Transient Receptor Potential (TRP) channels are an example. These are calcium or magnesium permissive channels. They are mainly expressed in peripheral nerve terminals and keratinocytes, where they participate in thermo-sensation.

items.item_title: Which ion channels are known to be sensitive to heat?
items.text: Four Transient Receptor Potential Vanilloid (TRPV) channels are associated with sensing heat. TRPV1 was the first identified in this family. It is expressed in keratinocytes and pain nervous fibres. TRPV1 acts as a sensor for noxious heat, with temperatures above 42°C triggering its activation. This leads to an entry of $\\text{Ca}^{2+}$. Pro-inflammatory signals can also make the channel open at 37°C. TRPV2 is sensitive to temperatures above 52°C and is expressed in fast pain transmission fibres. Two other heat receptors have been identified. These are TRPV3, which responds to temperatures above 34-38°C, and TRPV4, which responds to temperatures above 27-35°C.

items.item_title: What are the identified cold-sensitive ion channels?
items.text: Following the identification of heat receptors, a search for cold-sensitive receptors was conducted. TRPM8 was identified as one of these receptors. This channel responds to temperatures that range from 25°C to 28°C. It is also known to be activated by menthol or eucalyptol. The other identified cold receptor is TRPA1. TRPA1 responds to temperatures below 17°C. It is noted that this channel is also activated by wasabi or mustard oil. The burning-like sensation from spicy oil is said to resemble the sensation triggered by noxious cold. Both TRPM8 and TRPA1 are expressed in pain nervous fibres.

items.item_title: How does temperature regulate other types of calcium channels?
items.text: Other calcium channels are regulated indirectly by temperature. Two types of calcium ion channels exist: voltage-gated and ligand-gated. These channels are involved in processes like muscle contraction, neurotransmission, and cell differentiation. Among the voltage-gated channels, the T-type $\\text{Ca}^{2+}$ channels have been shown to respond to both temperature and voltage. Orai channels, which are calcium release-activated calcium channels, are also regulated by temperature. Their activation is controlled by STIM proteins. STIM proteins sense both $\\text{Ca}^{2+}$ levels and temperature. When temperatures are elevated (above 40°C), STIM proteins concentrate at the cell membrane but do not interact with Orai channels, which prevents $\\text{Ca}^{2+}$ entry. When the temperature cools, binding occurs, and calcium can enter the cell.

page_title: Worm Immobilization : Using Microfluidic Device

items.item_title: What are the stated benefits of using microfluidics for worm immobilization?
items.text: Microfluidic immobilization is presented as a method that provides consistent results and faster handling of C. elegans. This approach is used because other procedures have limitations. For example, the use of chemical compounds can induce physiological modifications. These changes might interfere with the biological question being investigated. Other techniques may result in worms that do not have an identical orientation. They also may not be accessible for subsequent manipulations. Microfluidics is shown to provide unidirectional orientation and prevent the superposition of worms. It also enables further actions, such as drug injection or the application of temperature control.

items.item_title: How does the passive "worm trap" method function?
items.text: This method is based on the mechanical trapping of C. elegans. The microfluidic chip contains parallel microchannels, which is convenient for processing many worms. The worm enters a microchannel through the action of liquid flow. The width of this channel is reduced to a point where the worm becomes immobilized. This technique has specific features. Upon reversion of the flow, the worm can be retrieved. Drugs can also be delivered through the liquid flow. Worms can be oriented in the same direction. It is noted that residual movement may occur with this method. However, this movement does not appear to be limiting, as it can be compensated for with appropriate imaging software.

items.item_title: What is the compressive method for worm immobilization?
items.text: This technique utilizes the physical properties of a PDMS membrane. In this configuration, the microfluidic device contains integrated valves. The C. elegans worm is positioned in the bottom flow layer channel. When pressure is applied, the membrane deflects. As a result of this deflection, the worm is physically immobilized. An adjustment to the pressure permits the use of different worm developmental stages. This method of immobilization may not be compatible with a long period of observation. The source also notes that this technique is not straightforward to implement. It is described as difficult to generate. It is suggested that biologists should use the passive method, as this active one is less simple to manage.

items.item_title: How does temperature-induced immobilization work in a microfluidic device?
items.text: This method, described in a 2008 paper by Chung et al., provides a strict immobilization of C. elegans. It is capable of stopping all internal movement. This makes it suitable for imaging sub-cellular structures. In this device, the worm is placed in the bottom flow channel. The flow and the worm's placement are controlled by two micro valves. A separate temperature-controlled liquid flow channel is positioned on top of the worm channel. This setup permits a rapid cool-down of the C. elegans temperature. While this method is noted as effective, it results in a total immobilization of the worm. Therefore, it is not considered adequate for live-animal video imaging. The cooling can be reversed with appropriate devices.

page_title: Survival Assays: Stress Resistance And Pathogenic Resistance In C. Elegans

items.item_title: Why are synchronized populations used in survival assays, and how are they obtained?
items.text: One noticeable advantage of C. elegans for survival assays is that synchronized, isogenic populations can be easily obtained. This is because the worm usually exists as a self-fertilizing hermaphrodite. Having age synchronization at the beginning of the experiments is a requirement. The method used for synchronization differs between solid and liquid systems. For solid cultures, the first step is to obtain a cohort of young adults. These are grown from eggs laid by gravid adults. For liquid culture systems, the method adopted is known as "bleaching". This technique is based on the fact that eggs are able to survive exposure to sodium hypochlorite.

items.item_title: How are lifespan assays conducted for C. elegans in different culture systems?
items.text: Lifespan assays are used for studying aging. They involve counting live and dead worms in synchronized groups at set intervals. Two types of systems are typically used: solid and liquid. Solid culture systems use agar-based hydrogels, and E. coli bacteria are provided as food. A worm is identified as dead if it does not react to a gentle touch from a sterilized platinum wire. This approach has limitations, such as the worms crawling off the plate or the plate desiccating. Liquid culture systems use a buffer supplemented with E. coli. These systems are suited for screening diet components or chemical treatments. In liquid, dead worms are identified as those that show no spontaneous movement or response to shaking.

items.item_title: What is the purpose of abiotic stress resistance assays and what factors are studied?
items.text: The capability to respond to a specific stress is measured. This can provide information regarding the interaction between internal and external stresses. It also provides information on biological processes at the cellular and subcellular level. Stress conditions are induced by several types of abiotic elements. These include oxidative stress, hypoxia, hyperoxia, heat, cold, and osmotic stress. Ultraviolet (UV) radiation, endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress, and heavy metal contamination are also studied. For instance, high temperatures are known to cause cellular disorders, such as neuronal degeneration. A typical temperature used for acute heat stress assays is 35°C. Hyperosmotic shock is used for osmotic stress tests, which induces water efflux and protein aggregation.

items.item_title: How are bacterial pathogen resistance assays performed with C. elegans?
items.text: C. elegans is used as a model host in pathogen resistance assays. These studies help in understanding pathogenesis and immunity. Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most popular model pathogen used. This bacterium kills worms through two distinct mechanisms: a slow method involving colonization of the intestine, or a fast method through the secretion of lethal toxins. The slow-killing assays are the most popular type of survival experiment using this pathogen. Several specific considerations are noted for these assays. Roper cultivation temperatures and fresh bacterial lawns are required for reliable results. Worms in the proper developmental stages, such as young adults or L4 larvae, must be used. FUdR treatment is recommended to prevent internal hatching. Dead worms must be counted frequently, every 6 to 12 hours.

page_title: C. elegans Mitosis Control With Thermosensitive Mutants

items.item_title: What is a thermosensitive mutant?
items.text: A thermosensitive mutant, also called ts, is one where a protein's activity is dependent on the temperature. The protein is functional at what is known as a "permissive" temperature. It becomes inactivated when the temperature is changed to a "restrictive" level. An example is the CYK-4 ts mutant in C. elegans. This specific protein is functional at 16°C. If the temperature is raised to over 25°C, the CYK-4 protein is inactivated. This characteristic allows researchers to control the protein's activity by changing the sample's temperature. Such mutants are used in experiments to study the specific function of proteins during various cellular processes, such as division.

items.item_title: What is the function of the CYK-4 protein during C. elegans embryo division?
items.text: The CYK-4 protein has a specific role during the division of C. elegans embryos. It has been identified as being required for the completion of cytokinesis. Cytokinesis is the process where the cell's cytoplasm divides, leading to two separate daughter cells. It is important to note what the protein is not required for. The CYK-4 protein is not needed for the initial steps of this process. These steps include the formation of the cleavage furrow. It is also not required to initiate the ingression of the furrow. Its function is specifically related to the final completion of the cytokinesis stage.

items.item_title: What happens to a CYK-4 ts mutant embryo when its temperature is shifted?
items.text: When CYK-4 thermosensitive mutant embryos are shifted from the permissive temperature to the restrictive one, a specific effect is observed. Experiments have been done by changing the temperature of the embryo samples from 16°C to 25°C. This shift to the higher, restrictive temperature causes the CYK-4 protein to become inactivated. For embryos that are in the process of dividing, this inactivation results in cytokinesis arrest. The process of cell division is blocked. Observations from such experiments have shown the cleavage furrow, which forms and pulls inwards at 16°C, actually regresses when the temperature is shifted to 25°C. This demonstrates that the protein's function is stopped, preventing the completion of division.

items.item_title: How was temperature control used to study these mutant embryos?
items.text: The CYK-4 thermosensitive (ts) mutant embryos were studied by using a specific experimental setup. The embryos were examined under a microscope. A temperature control platform was used with the microscope. This system permitted the temperature of the embryo samples to be selectively modified. For these experiments, the temperature was changed between the permissive level of 16°C and the restrictive level of 25°C. This precise temperature change allowed for the inactivation of the CYK-4 protein at a specific time. The observed result of this temperature shift was the blocking of cytokinesis. This method of controlling temperature allows researchers to study the effects of inactivating a protein during a dynamic cellular process.

page_title: Membranes Integrated in Microfluidic Devices

items.item_title: What is a membrane in a microfluidic context?
items.text: A membrane is described as a semi-permeable barrier. It is used in microfluidic systems. Its purpose is to control the transport of molecules and species within the system. Other terms, such as "filter," "sieve," or "porous support," may be found in literature instead of "membrane". The term "filter" is applied because the membrane permits the separation of particles based on their different sizes. Membranes can also be employed for surface patterning, where they act as a shadow mask. Additionally, a membrane can be used during the fabrication of 3D microfluidic devices. In this application, it is able to interconnect two microfluidic channels, one above and one below the membrane.

items.item_title: What forces cause transport across a membrane?
items.text: Membranes are utilized to control transport. A distinction is made between "separation," which is transport out of the system, and "contacting," which is transport into the system. This transport is caused by a chemical gradient. Several types of gradients can be the cause of this movement. Examples include a temperature gradient or a difference in pressure. A difference in concentration can also be the cause. An electrical potential difference is another example of a chemical gradient that results in transport. The specific application determines which mode of transport is used and what gradient is present.

items.item_title: What is the difference between dense and porous membranes?
items.text: The morphology of the membrane is important for transport, and two kinds are distinguished. Dense membranes are permeable to single molecules. With these, transport occurs in the material itself, not through holes. The permeability ($P$) of a dense membrane is calculated as the product of diffusivity ($D$) and solubility ($S$). As a result, dense membranes are considered material dependent. The second type is the porous membrane. For porous membranes, transport happens within the pores of the membrane. This transport is therefore dependent on the surface and volume porosity, the distribution of pore sizes, and the tortuosity. Tortuosity is a parameter that corrects for the deviation of pore shapes from perfect cylinders. Porous membranes are also characterized by retention ($R$), which ranges from 0 (no retention) to 1 (full retention), and the MWCO coefficient.

items.item_title: How are membranes typically integrated into microfluidic chips?
items.text: Several methods for integrating membranes into microfluidic devices have been performed. The first method is the direct incorporation of the membrane into the chip. This can be done by either clamping or gluing the membrane onto a layer or between two layers. While clamping is the simplest way, it has disadvantages, as fluids may be sucked in between the cover plates. If glue is used, it can easily fill the pores of the membrane. This filling of the pores blocks the transport. A method to overcome these problems is the use of micro stereo-lithography. In this process, a 3D chip is prepared from a photo-curable liquid polymer using a UV beam. The membrane is put in a precursor solution, which eliminates the need for a separate sealing step. After the chip is prepared, the non-crossed polymers are washed away.

page_title: C. elegans Immobilization Via Microfluidics: A Short Review

items.item_title: Why is immobilization needed for C. elegans imaging, and what are the issues with conventional techniques?
items.text: Immobilization is a prerequisite for achieving high-resolution, live-cell videos and images of C. elegans. This is necessary because contingent movements can give rise to artifacts or out-of-focus images, which makes the entire observation useless. Conventional methods are divided into chemical and physical techniques. Chemical methods, such as using metal ions, can adversely affect the cells, even at low concentrations. Physical techniques, like the use of glue or anesthetic drugs, are well-established but can potentially induce physiological modifications in the sample. These methods may also prevent future manipulation. Furthermore, they are low-throughput methods, as it is difficult and time-consuming to immobilize a large number of nematodes this way.

items.item_title: How is C. elegans immobilized using cooling in a microfluidic device?
items.text: A method for temperature-induced immobilization was developed by Kwanghun Chung and colleagues. This technique employs a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic chip that has built-in valves to control a suspension of nematodes. The protocol involves loading a worm, reversibly immobilizing it by cooling, and then scanning it with a high-resolution microscope. Nematodes are cooled to approximately 4°C to immobilize them. This procedure does not use any anesthetic. It has been proven to be as effective as immobilization by sodium azide, but it does not have the potential side effects associated with drug use. The system also includes features for high-precision positioning, which allows each animal to be placed in the same location automatically.

items.item_title: What mechanical methods are used for on-chip worm immobilization?
items.text: Two methods that use physical force are mechanical trapping and compression. One device, designed by S. Elizabeth Hulme and colleagues, uses an array of wedge-shaped channels, or "clamps". These clamps are able to mechanically trap large numbers of worms at once. The microchannels narrow gradually, which allows them to accommodate worms of different sizes. A constant pressure difference is required to drive and trap the worms. This method does not disturb the natural biochemical state of the worm. A second method, developed by Trushal V. Chokshi, uses compressive immobilization. In this device, a high-pressure (25 psi) air flow causes a deformable PDMS membrane to deflect onto the worm, inhibiting its movements. The immobilization time for this compressive method is rather short, lasting minutes.

items.item_title: How can carbon dioxide be used to stop the worm from moving in a microfluidic chip?
items.text: Carbon dioxide (CO2) is able to inhibit a nematode's movement for long periods, sometimes up to two hours. This method is reversible, allowing the worm to recover a few seconds after the process stops, and it is suitable for different age groups. The technique, developed by T. V. Chokshi and colleagues use a two-layer PDMS microfluidic chip. Pure CO2 is allowed to flow through the control layer of the chip. PDMS is highly permeable to non-polar gases. The carbon dioxide diffuses through the membrane separating the layers. It then replaces the air in the immobilization chamber, creating a CO2 micro-environment that immobilizes the worm. This lack of oxygen also reduces the photobleaching of fluorescent markers, making the method appealing for long-term fluorescence imaging.

page_title: Human Cells: Cellular Metabolism

items.item_title: How do human cells generally respond to a decrease in temperature?
items.text: The regulation of body temperature is a major physiological function in humans. At the cellular level, cells react to a temperature decrease with several changes. A general decrease in metabolism is observed. The fluidity of the cell membrane is also lessened. Other cellular processes are slowed. These include transcriptional activity and the rate of protein synthesis. The rate of vesicular transport is also decreased in response to colder conditions. Mitochondria, which are the centre of cell metabolism, are involved in regulating and maintaining the temperature of cells. These organelles are very sensitive to temperature.

items.item_title: What is the function of mitochondria in cellular metabolism?
items.text: Mitochondria are specialised organelles and are known as the metabolic centre of the cell. They are involved in important biochemical pathways that generate chemical energy for the cell. Mitochondria also participate in other processes, such as cell death pathways, phospholipid metabolism, and calcium sensing. Their function is very sensitive to temperature, and they participate in cellular thermogenesis. In pancreatic beta-cells, for instance, mitochondria link glucose metabolism to the release of insulin. The ATP produced by mitochondria binds to and closes K+ channels. This action leads to the opening of Ca2+ ion channels, and the resulting entry of Ca2+ into the cell leads to the release of insulin vesicles.

items.item_title: How does cold-induced thermogenesis work in brown adipose tissue?
items.text: In mammals, brown adipose tissue is involved in maintaining body temperature. When the temperature is low, sympathetic nerves stimulate brown adipocytes. This stimulation occurs through the release of norepinephrine, which binds to beta-adrenergic receptors on the cell surface. This action starts a signalling cascade. The cascade leads to the rapid activation of UCP-1, an uncoupling protein. UCP-1 is located at the mitochondrial inner membrane. It functions by uncoupling the proton gradient from the production of ATP. This uncoupling results in heat production instead of energy storage. This protein is required for cold-induced thermogenesis in brown adipocytes and is specific to this cell type.

items.item_title: What considerations are important when imaging mitochondria?
items.text: Choosing the correct fluorescent marker is an important consideration. This choice depends on the type of experiment being performed. Mitochondria are known to be very sensitive to cell stress. For this reason, it is best to avoid the transient transfection of fluorescent proteins, as this is a source of stress for the cells. A better method is to establish stable cell lines that express a fluorescent version of the protein of interest. If the experiments require long-term imaging, a fluorescent marker with a wavelength that is not strongly energetic should be chosen. This precaution helps to prevent mito chondrial damage. For short-term experiments on live cells (less than 45 minutes), fluorescent dyes that are taken up by mitochondria may be useful, such as Mitotracker green.

page_title: Human Cells: Cell Cycle Temperature Control

items.item_title: What are the main phases of the mammalian cell cycle?
items.text: The mammalian cell cycle is composed of four distinct phases. These are the G1 (Gap 1), S (Synthesis), G2 (Gap 2), and M (Mitosis) phases. To progress from one phase to the next, the cell must pass through internal checkpoints. These checkpoints ensure that the DNA material and the mitotic machinery are ready for cell division. The G1 phase is a period of cell growth where proteins for DNA replication are accumulated. In the S phase, DNA is replicated, doubling the cell's DNA content. The cell then enters the G2 phase to prepare for mitosis. Finally, the M phase consists of four steps (prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase) where chromosomes are separated into two daughter cells.

items.item_title: How is the progression through the cell cycle controlled?
items.text: Progression through the cell cycle is controlled by specialized intra-cellular proteins. Cyclin and Cyclin-Dependent Kinases (CDKs) form active complexes that are present during all phases. In G1, Cyclin D expression depends on extra-cellular mitogenic signals and associates with CDK4 and CDK6. These complexes then phosphorylate the retinoblastoma (Rb) protein. When Rb is phosphorylated, it releases the E2F transcription factor, which becomes active. E2F target genes include Cyclin E. Cyclin E interacts with CDK2, an action that promotes the G1 to S phase transition. Later, the Cyclin A-Cdk2 complex is active during the S phase. This is followed by Cyclin B-Cdk1 activity in the G2/M phase, which promotes the breakdown of the nuclear envelope and initiates mitosis. These complexes are also regulated by two types of inhibitors.

items.item_title: How does temperature affect the cell cycle in mammalian cells?
items.text: The cell cycle, and the mitosis phase in particular, is dependent on temperature. Early research on HeLa cells showed that temperature controls the rate and speed at which a cell divides. The mitosis phase was found to be very sensitive to temperature changes. For instance, cell cultures subjected to temperatures between 24°C and 31°C exhibited an accumulation of cells in the mitosis phase. The length of the M-phase is generally longer at low temperatures. This is likely due to a decrease in enzymatic activity. High temperatures (42-44°C) also affect division. At these temperatures, spindle assembly is impaired. This impairment means the cells do not meet the requirements to proceed through the spindle assembly checkpoint.

items.item_title: How are temperature-sensitive mutants used to study the mammalian cell cycle?
items.text: The use of temperature-sensitive mutants is less common in mammalian cell studies than in yeast or C. elegans. However, they are still applied. One example is the CHO-K1 thermosenstive TM3 mutant. This mutant exhibits a block in the S/G2 phase. It also shows chromosome instability. These effects were found to be caused by a mutation in Uba1. Uba1 is a ubiquitin-activating enzyme. Further analysis using Fucci fluorescence showed that Uba1 was necessary for the degradation of geminin. This degradation must occur for the cell to progress from the S phase to the G2 phase. This demonstrates how these mutants can be used to identify the function of specific proteins within the cell cycle.

page_title: The importance of seal chambers to avoid evaporation while doing live-cell imaging with thermalization

items.item_title: Why is media evaporation a problem during long-term live-cell imaging?
items.text: Media evaporation is a main issue for long-term analyses. This problem is especially pronounced when the medium volume for the biological sample is very small, such as a few micro-litters. Evaporation is a serious factor. It can greatly affect cell happiness and, if not controlled, cell viability. The main method suggested to avoid medium evaporation is the use of sealed chambers. This method also prevents ensuing changes in osmotic pressure. Other proposals have mentioned using a sandwich of cover slips to hold the sample. Another mentioned option is a seal chamber that has the option for medium renewal.

items.item_title: What limitations are mentioned for some other thermalization systems?
items.text: Other systems exist that allow for thermalization and humidity control. The two main systems mentioned are stage incubators and thermalization chambers that are placed around the microscope. A limitation is associated with the majority of these systems. They often face the constraint of not permitting direct access to the sample being imaged. The main method for avoiding media evaporation and the resulting changes in osmotic pressure is to use sealed chambers. Some systems provide the option to work with these closed and sealed chambers. Other suggestions include using a sandwich of cover slips or a sealed chamber that allows for medium renewal.

items.item_title: How was an experiment set up to test the efficiency of a microscopy chamber at avoiding evaporation?
items.text: A test was developed to check the capability of a closed chamber to avoid evaporation over a long period. The sample mounting system used in the test respects microscopy standards for an inverted setup. It is composed of three pieces: a cover slip, a spacer, and a thermalizing unit. The spacer is re-usable and approximately 500µm thick. The thermalizing unit is placed on top, which creates a sealed chamber. These parts are held in an insert. Two different, fully biocompatible spacers were tested: A and B. Spacer A was a standard, commercially available silicon sheet. Spacer B resulted from in-house developments. A coloured liquid was used to mimic the sample medium and for ease of visualisation.

items.item_title: What were the results of the long-term evaporation test on the two different spacers?
items.text: The evolution of the medium inside chamber A and chamber B was observed to be very different. Images were taken over 65 hours to make the evaporation process visual. Inside chamber A, which used the standard silicon spacer, the medium evaporated by more than 90 percent after 16 hours and 30 minutes. After 65 hours, there was no medium available in chamber A, leaving just the concentrated colorant. The results for chamber B were different. The evolution in Chamber B was determined to be compatible with long-term imaging needs. After 16 hours, the drop was still present. At the end of the 65-hour test, only half of the medium in chamber B had evaporated. This showed that spacer B provides better sealing.

page_title: Cell Lines: Vesicular Trafficking

items.item_title: What is vesicular trafficking?
items.text: Vesicular trafficking is a process that spans from the cellular membrane to the cell's organelles. Cells interact with their surroundings by secreting or importing proteins. These proteins are packaged in vesicles. At the membrane, endocytosis and exocytosis permit communication with the environment. These actions also ensure the renewal of receptors, ion channels, surface proteins, and membrane lipids. Inside the cell, an organized trafficking system ensures vesicles are directed to the correct place. In the cytoplasm, vesicles travel along microtubules. They use motor proteins to shuttle to different organelles.

items.item_title: How does clathrin-mediated endocytosis function?
items.text: This is one of the most detailed pathways for endocytosis, which allows the cell to internalize substances. The process begins when a ligand binds to its receptor on the cell surface. A signal is then sent inside the cell to recruit clathrin proteins. Clathrin proteins are arranged in a triskelion shape. These triskelions bind to adaptor proteins on the forming vesicle. They function by mechanically deforming the membrane. This action forms a rounded vesicle. The membrane invaginates into a small coated pit, which then grows into a coated vesicle. A GTPase protein called dynamin ensures the vesicle separates from the membrane. Dynamin forms a helix around the vesicle's neck to pinch it off. After separation, the clathrin proteins disassemble from the vesicle. The vesicle is then shuttled to the early endosome.

items.item_title: What are the main steps of exocytosis?
items.text: Exocytosis is a process that allows for the secretion of proteins, hormones, and neurotransmitters. It is also involved in antigen presentation and the turnover of plasma membrane components. The process involves four steps: approach, tethering, docking, and fusion. First, vesicles approach the plasma membrane, using motor proteins like kinesin or dynein that move on a microtubule track. Second, the vesicle loosely attaches to the membrane in a step called tethering. This occurs when Rab-GTP on the vesicle binds to a Rab effector protein at the target membrane. Third and fourth, vesicle docking and fusion are managed by SNARE proteins. There is a v-SNARE (vesicle SNARE) and t-SNAREs (target SNAREs). The binding of the v-SNARE to the t-SNAREs causes the fusion of the vesicle to the plasma membrane.

items.item_title: What is the function of motor proteins in vesicular transport?
items.text: Vesicles are actively transported within the cytoplasm by motor proteins. These proteins are described as molecular motors. They use microtubules as intracellular tracks to move along. Motor proteins provide a mechanical force to ensure vesicle transport, a process that is fueled by ATP hydrolysis. The two main types of these proteins are kinesin and dynein. Kinesin proteins move from the minus end of the microtubules toward the plus end. This protein's function was studied in neurons, where it transports synaptic vesicles along the axon. Dynein transports vesicles in the opposite direction, from the plus end toward the minus end. This direction of movement is necessary for retrograde transport in neurons.

page_title: Human cells: Microfluidics as a tool for drug delivery

items.item_title: Why is microfluidics considered a useful tool for drug delivery?
items.text: Microfluidics, the science of fluid flows at the microscale, is seen as an answer for more effective and targeted drug administration. Conventional methods, such as oral administering or injection, have a main drawback. The drug's pathway from the inoculation site to the area of interest is often too long, which can make the treatment potentially ineffective. Microfluidic devices, like a lab-on-a-chip, can provide a platform for both drug synthesis and delivery. Using these devices for drug administration also has other advantages. These include the reduction of both pain and the risk of side effects. Further improvements are also noted in cost, user-friendliness, safety, and portability.

items.item_title: How can microfluidics be used to study drug effects at the cellular level?
items.text: One possibility offered by microfluidics is the ability to study a drug's effects at the cellular level within various physicochemical microenvironments. Cell culture platforms based on microfluidics can replicate in vivo conditions and generate different environmental profiles. The technology allows for precise and controlled drug flow to the culture chamber. This makes it possible to monitor cellular responses to high concentrations or other biochemical stimuli. Microfluidic gradient generators (MGGs) are often employed for this purpose. These devices offer advantages over conventional alternatives, such as Transwell chambers. The noted benefits include higher resolution, real-time observation, tunable drug concentration, and reduced costs.

items.item_title: What are "smart particles" in the context of drug delivery?
items.text: Microfluidics knowhow is used in the manufacturing of "smart particles". This term describes micro- or nano-sized particles that are used as drug carriers. They are intended for high-precision and localized drug delivery to unhealthy tissues. These particles are classified into three types. Biocapsules are used to encapsulate transplanted tissue in a biodegradable, semi-permeable particle to avoid rejection by the immune system. Microparticles are used to improve the efficacy of oral or intra-venous drug administration by carrying the agent in biodegradable polymers. Nanoparticles, which range in size from 5 to 1000 nanometres, are used in neoplasia therapy. They exploit the abnormal permeability of cancerous tissue, allowing them to bind to it and release the therapeutic agent.

items.item_title: What types of microneedles are used for drug administration?
items.text: Needles can be miniaturised to create microneedles, which improve delivery effectiveness and reduce the pain associated with injection. These are divided into four general types. Solid microneedles are used to puncture the skin; the needle is then removed, and a drug formulation like a patch or cream is applied. Coated microneedles are covered with a water-soluble formulation of the drug. After puncturing the skin, the drug dissolves off, and the microneedle can be removed. Dissolvable microneedles are made from soluble or biodegradable polymers that encapsulate the drug. They dissolve completely within the skin to release the agent. Finally, hollow microneedles possess built-in channels, allowing a drug to be infused or to diffuse into the skin.

items.item_title: What is an "organ-on-a-chip" and how is it used for drug development?
items.text: An "organ-on-a-chip" is another application of microfluidics. Researchers have succeeded in building microfluidic devices that can replicate entire biological systems, such as human organs and arteries. These devices are able to reproduce the in vivo microenvironments of the organs. This capability provides a low-cost platform for preclinical testing. The technique that allows this is called compartmentalisation. Multicompartment, microfluidic-based devices can be used to improve the drug development process. By creating a more accurate model of a human organ, these chips can be used for low-cost drug screening before moving to more expensive trials.

page_title: Human cells: pH monitoring in microfluidics

items.item_title: Why is pH monitoring a concern for cell culture in microfluidics?
items.text: Monitoring the microenvironment in microfluidic chips is a known issue. Cells are enclosed by the chip, which prevents direct observation of their surroundings. The pH level is one of the parameters that must be probed inside the culture chambers. Cells are known to influence their environment. For example, carbon dioxide and lactate are produced during respiration, and these substances acidify the microenvironment. In a typical microfluidic chamber, the amount of liquid is extremely low, often measured in microlitres. The effects of these natural cellular processes are magnified because of these small volumes. For these reasons, the ability to dynamically measure and potentially control the pH level is a necessary field of study for microfluidic applications.

items.item_title: How is optical pH monitoring adapted for microfluidic devices?
items.text: Many cell culture media contain pH indicators, such as phenol red, which change colour based on the environment's pH level. This allows for simple, though rough, monitoring. Quantitative measurements are difficult because the path for light absorption in microfluidic devices is very short. A technique developed by Magnusson et al. overcomes this. It uses standard laboratory equipment, like a transmitted-light microscope and a CCD camera. The method requires calibration with a non-absorbing liquid. It functions by including a non-absorbing empty area of the chip in the imaging frame. This empty area is used to correct for variations in the light source intensity over time. The ratio of absorption at two different wavelengths (matching the indicator's acid and basic forms) is calculated. The pH value is then derived from the natural logarithm of this ratio.

items.item_title: How does an electrolyte-insulator-semiconductor (EIS) sensor measure pH?
items.text: A microfluidic device capable of monitoring both pH and glucose was developed by Lin et al.. This hybrid device uses an electrolyte-insulator-semiconductor (EIS) sensor. The sensor is bonded to a glass substrate at the bottom of the chip. Two PDMS layers are used for fluidic handling, one for channels and one containing pneumatic pumps that also work as valves. A reference electrode is placed on a glass substrate at the top. The measurement relies on the flat band voltage of the EIS sensor. This voltage is defined by an expression in which the surface potential is the only free parameter. This surface potential ($\\Psi$) is exclusively determined by changes in the pH level of the surrounding environment. Consequently, the variation of the flat band potential is used to measure the pH.

items.item_title: How can pH be actively controlled, not just measured, in a microfluidic chip?
items.text: A device developed by Welch and Christen (2014) is able to monitor pH and also adjust the level automatically. This system uses an ion-selective-field effect-transistor (ISFET) for the pH measurement, combined with a PDMS chip to manage the fluids. The PDMS chip contains two reservoirs, which store a basic (pH \+) solution and an acid (pH \-) solution. The output of these two chambers is regulated by two valves. These valves control the flow and the subsequent mixing of the solutions. This process permits active control over the pH of the mixed solution before it reaches the ISFET sensor. A software, which uses a proportional-derivate feedback algorithm, controls the valves based on the readouts from the sensor to manage the pH level in real-time.

items.item_title: How can hydrogels be used to autonomously regulate pH-dependent flow?
items.text: Hydrogels are polymer matrices that can trap water molecules. A feature of these materials is their ability to change morphology (shape and size) in response to environmental variations, such as pH, without needing an external power supply. This capability was used by Atwe et al. to design a microfluidic device. The device uses hydrogels as a micro-valve to control flows based on their pH. The pH-sensitive hydrogel contains acid or basic groups. Ionization of these groups, which is triggered by the pH of the medium, leads to a flow of counter-ions into the gel. This is followed by a flow of water to equalize osmotic pressure, causing the hydrogel to expand. In the device, hydrogel slices are packaged within the microchannels, allowing the flow path to autonomously narrow or expand based on the medium's pH.

page_title: Gradients generation for cell biology in microfluidics

items.item_title: Why is the in vitro study of chemical gradients relevant to cell biology?
items.text: Chemical gradients are studied because they regulate a number of cellular functions in vivo. Gradient-dependent phenomena are found in nature. One example is chemotaxis, which is cell migration started by a gradient. It has been shown that chemical gradients affect various cell behaviours. These behaviours include cell migration, proliferation, and differentiation during development. They are also involved in wound healing, inflammation, and tumorgenesis. For these reasons, it is evident that recreating these conditions in vitro is a necessary part of life science studies. Microfluidics, the science of engineering fluid streams at a small scale, provides tools to recreate and control these gradients.

items.item_title: What is one drawback of using laminar flow to generate gradients in microfluidics?
items.text: One of the most used techniques for generating gradients in microfluidics involves exploiting laminar flows. This method is simple, but it has one main disadvantage. The disadvantage is the unavoidable creation of convective flows. Convection can lead to artifacts. This is because it may not be a condition experienced by cells in vivo. Convection may also imply shear stress and other related problems for the cells. These problems can include the removal of cell secretions, non-isotropous migration, and asymmetrical mass transport. These effects interfere with the study of cell behaviour.

items.item_title: How does the "microfluidic palette" device create gradients without convective flows?
items.text: A device called the "microfluidic palette" was designed to deliver soluble factors without shear stress. It generates controlled gradients by decoupling convective flows from diffusion within the device's main chamber. This is achieved without using any membrane or gel. The principle involves matching the bulk flow rates through the inlet and the outlet. This is shown in a cross-channel generator where one unit carries a solute and the other carries a buffer. This setup allows a gradient to be created across the microchannel by diffusion. In the full two-dimensional device, several convection units are placed around a circular microchamber. These units act as a perfect sink or source for a solute. The matching of bulk flow is achieved by balancing the pressure between all the access ports.

items.item_title: How can non-linear gradient profiles be generated in a microfluidic device?
items.text: Non-linear concentration gradients are observed in biological processes, such as in breast cancer cells responding to EGF. A device capable of generating these non-linear gradients was developed by Selimovic et al. This device does not require cumbersome external control elements. It works by injecting two liquids, containing different concentrations of chemicals, into a single microfluidic channel. This is done using a standard syringe pump. This main microchannel then branches off into several side channels. These side channels lead to an asymmetrical channel grid, which serves as a storage region. This grid acts as a source for 512 storage wells, which fill with different mixtures of the two solutions. The asymmetric geometry, combined with diffusion, results in a one-dimensional concentration gradient in the side branches.

page_title: Asymmetric Cell Division in C. elegans

items.item_title: What is asymmetric cell division and why is it important?
items.text: Asymmetric cell division is a process observed when one cell divides to produce two daughter cells that have distinct cellular fates and functions. The resulting daughter cells may also be different in size. This mechanism is considered essential for generating cell lineage diversity. It also creates specialised functions in multicellular organisms. The process is found in many types of organisms, including yeast, bacteria, flies, and higher vertebrates. In mammals, this type of division is central to development. For example, stem cells, such as neural progenitors, can divide asymmetrically. This allows for both self-renewal of the stem cell and the creation of a progeny of differentiated daughter cells.

items.item_title: How is asymmetric cell division generated?
items.text: Two main mechanisms are described that generate asymmetric cell division. The first mechanism is the asymmetric distribution of cell components within the daughter cells. These components are also known as cell fate determinants. The second mechanism involves the asymmetric localisation of the daughter cells. This localisation is relative to external factors. An example of such an external factor is signalling that comes from neighbour cells. Research in model organisms like C. elegans and D. melanogaster has helped to decipher the molecular mechanisms involved. For instance, Numb was identified in Drosophila as the first asymmetrically distributed cell fate determinant.

items.item_title: How are the main body axes established in the C. elegans embryo?
items.text: A succession of asymmetric divisions during the development of the worm ensures the establishment of the organism's axes. The very first cellular division in the embryo is asymmetric. This process begins at fertilisation. The sperm centrosome is pushed towards one side of the embryo. This position defines where the first mitotic spindle will be and also sets the division site. At the end of this first division, the anterior-posterior axis is organised. The originally symmetric P0 embryo is partitioned into a larger anterior blastomere (AB) and a smaller posterior one (P1). The dorso-ventral axis is defined during the second division by EMS and Abp. Finally, the left-right asymmetry is set up at the end of the third division.

items.item_title: What is the function of PAR proteins in C. elegans asymmetric division?
items.text: PAR proteins have an important function in the determination of cell polarity. In C. elegans mutants for PAR, a loss of anterior-posterior polarity was observed, along with defective cell fate destiny. Before the first division of the embryo, these PAR proteins are distributed equally at the cell cortex. Their repositioning is necessary for establishing polarity. This repositioning is facilitated by flows of the cortical acto-myosin network. This same acto-myosin network is also needed for positioning the sperm centrosome, which occurs before the first division. Before division occurs, cell fate determinants like polarity proteins are unequally localised to the cell poles, establishing cell polarization. The orientation of the cell division plane will depend on these polarized poles.

items.item_title: Why are temperature-sensitive mutants used to investigate asymmetric division?
items.text: C. elegans embryos are suitable for live-cell imaging because they are large and immotile. A large collection of temperature-sensitive (ts) mutants is also available for this organism. These mutants have been an invaluable tool for deciphering the molecular pathways that control asymmetric division. For instance, a genetic screen was used to identify ts cell-division mutants that were defective for spindle-orientation, cytokinesis, or had abnormal microtubule organisation. The use of these ts mutants involves shifting the worms from a permissive temperature to a restrictive one. This temperature shift permits an inducible and reversible inactivation of the protein being studied. This allows for the observation of rapid downstream effects while the embryo is being imaged.

page_title: Drosophila: The Fruit Fly Model

items.item_title: Why is D. melanogaster used in research?
items.text: D. melanogaster is often used as a model organism. This is because it is easy to manipulate and grow in laboratory conditions. These conditions apply to all stages of its life cycle, from egg to adult. The fruit fly has a developmental cycle that is dependent on temperature. At 25°C, this cycle takes 8.5 to 9 days. Growth is slowed at higher and lower temperatures. The flies also show sexual dimorphism, which allows for easy identification of males and females. Females are larger than males, and there are differences in abdominal patterning and colour. Males also possess claspers and sexcomb bristles. The model is used in genetic, developmental, and behavioural studies.

items.item_title: What makes the fruit fly useful for genetic studies?
items.text: The fruit fly is a suitable model for deciphering gene function. This is due to the availability of multiple genetic tools. It is easy to generate stable genetic stocks through crosses and selection. Effective genetic screens using mutagenesis or transposon insertion for gene disruption can be performed. Other available tools include genetic transformation, RNAi for conditional knock-down, and the CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing technology. The GAL4/UAS system is also available, which allows for conditional gene expression. Drosophila has a small genome of 180Mb with about 13600 genes. This genome has been fully sequenced. A global picture of gene expression, DNA replication, and chromatin regulation throughout development has been established.

items.item_title: How is sex determined in Drosophila?
items.text: The genome of D. melanogaster is distributed in four chromosomes. These consist of three autosomes and one pair of sex chromosomes. The study of these sex chromosomes has contributed to the understanding of sex determination. In humans, the presence of the Y chromosome determines the male sex. This is not the case for flies. Instead, the dose of X chromosomes in the chromosome complement is responsible for male determination. The sensing of the double X chromosome is done at the cellular level. This sensing action then initiates a transcriptional cascade, which leads to the final sex determination.

items.item_title: How is the fruit fly used to study development and behaviour?
items.text: The use of melanogaster for developmental biology began with foundational discoveries of the Hox genes. These genes are responsible for pattern formation and the body plan. The whole process of transformation from an embryo to a complex animal is widely studied. This includes rapid divisions in the syncytial embryo, tissue development, larval growth, and the formation of adult structures from imaginal disks. Confocal microscopy is an important technology, as it allows for the imaging of fluorescently labelled tissues during these stages. Research on the adult fly is also conducted. The adult lifespan is around 30 days, though this varies with temperature. Many studies are linked to neurobiology. These include studies on sexual behaviour, sleep patterns, circadian clocks, and ageing.

page_title: C. elegans – The Need Of Controlling Temperature

items.item_title: Why is C. elegans used as a model organism in biology?
items.text: C. elegans has been used as a model organism for decades, beginning with research in the early 1960s into neuronal development. It has been the subject of over 20,000 publications, and research involving this nematode has been associated with five Nobel Prizes. The organism is considered simple, being formed of only 1000 cells. This simplicity is one reason it was the first animal to have its genome completely sequenced. Its neuronal connection network, or "connectome," has also been realised. In the laboratory, C. elegans is very easy to grow on a Petri dish, requiring only two incubators set at 15°C and 25°C. The growing time is also fast, at around 56 hours.

items.item_title: How is C. elegans development influenced by temperature?
items.text: Temperature has a pronounced effect on the development of C. elegans. The organism's growth development is sensitive to temperature. The life cycle includes four larval stages, L1 to L4, followed by a mature adult stage. The length of each of these cycles is dependent on the temperature. This dependency is quite clear. If embryos are grown at 15°C, they will reach the mature adult stage in six days. If the temperature is increased by 10°C to 25°C, this time is reduced by more than half. At the warmer temperature, they can become adult worms in just 2.5 days.

items.item_title: What is the "Dauer" developmental stage in C. elegans?
items.text: The "Dauer" stage is an additional developmental stage for the nematode. It is considered a dormant phase. This stage appears if the environment is not stable enough during the first two larval stages. During this dormant phase, the worm can survive for up to four months. This is a large increase compared to its normal lifespan of about three weeks. The appearance of this stage is related to the function of two genes, daf-2 and daf-23. When these genes are expressed, they permit the normal growing cycle to proceed. If they are not present, the nematode turns to the "Dauer" stage instead.

items.item_title: How is the aging process in C. elegans affected by temperature?
items.text: The lifespan of C. elegans is dependent on its environment. Temperature is a specific condition that influences aging. When grown in a liquid environment at 16°C, the worm's lifespan is 23 days. If the temperature is set to 25°C, this lifespan gets reduced to only 9 days. This difference is explained by the function of clock genes, or Clk. These genes control circadian rhythms, which are cycles of gene expression or behaviours that repeat approximately every 24 hours. These cycles represent an internal clock. This clock can be synchronised by external stimuli, which for C. elegans, are related to light/dark and temperature. Wild worms can adjust their lifespan according to the temperature, but mutant worms lacking the Clk gene cannot respond to it.

items.item_title: What is the application of temperature-sensitive C. elegans mutants in research?
items.text: Temperature-sensitive C. elegans worms are used to study the genes that act on growth and development. Using these mutants provides a way to avoid the lethal effect of certain zygotic or maternal effect mutations. One study identified 24 thermo-sensitive alleles for 13 different genes. A mutation like mex-1 was found to have direct effects on the development of embryo polarity. These mutants are also used to study some neurologic diseases, such as ALS and Huntington. For these conditions, temperature-sensitive mutant genes can work as modifiers for protein misfolding and for toxicity aggregation. This application gives the possibility to combine live-cell imaging with fast temperature shifts, watching GFP-tagged mutant protein aggregation as the temperature is changed from permissive to restrictive.

page_title: Temperature control and live-cell imaging

items.item_title: Why is it important to maintain a specific temperature during live-cell imaging?
items.text: Maintaining homeostatic temperatures is a necessary consideration for live imaging. The actual temperature of the cells being imaged is often lower than what is anticipated. It is important to correct this because even a difference of a couple of degrees matters. For example, many cell types will stall or even reverse the cell cycle when their temperature is 20–22°C. Furthermore, at temperatures between 23°C and 35°C, the timing of mitosis is dilated. This dilation indicates that a substantial stress is being placed on the cells, altering their normal function.

items.item_title: What often causes the temperature at the cell location to be lower than expected?
items.text: A main reason for lower-than-expected temperatures is the use of immersion objective lenses, particularly when they are used without an objective lens heater. The microscope itself acts as a large heat sink. This effect can cause a temperature differential of as much as 10°C between the chamber and the cells. Even when using dry objectives, a notable temperature differential can still exist. This difference in temperature is often observed between the edges of the specimen holder and the specific central area where the cells are actually being imaged.

items.item_title: How can the actual temperature at the cell location be measured?
items.text: It is necessary that the temperature is measured directly where the cells are growing, not just in the chamber holding them. This measurement can be accomplished with an inexpensive non-contact thermometer that is equipped with laser sighting, such as an OS-PAL. When using immersion objectives or on inverted microscopes where there is no line of sight, a different method is needed. A small thermocouple probe can be directly inserted at the focal point of the microscope. This can be safely done by attaching the small probe to the coverslip, where cells would normally be, and assembling the chamber as usual, including filling it with media or water.

items.item_title: What is stage drift and how is it related to heating?
items.text: Stage drift is a side-effect that can occur as a result of heating the microscope system above the ambient temperature. Microscopes are constructed mostly of metal components. These metal structures typically have little or no engineered thermal breaks. Because of this construction, the microscope acts as a large heat sink. This becomes especially problematic with stage-type heaters, although it can also affect other heating types. The change in temperature causes the metal to expand or contract, which results in a slow "drift" of the stage and the sample, moving it out of focus.

items.item_title: How can stage drift be managed during long-term imaging? To minimise the effect of temperature-related drift, the entire system should be allowed to equilibrate. This process should take approximately 45 to 60 minutes. The equilibration setup should include a mock specimen chamber and have the objective lens (if using immersion) in place and focused. While this procedure will help, it will not be sufficient to maintain the same focal plane for long-term filming. To solve this, all microscope manufacturers offer some type of focal correction. These systems are able to maintain the same focal plane for extended periods.

page_title: Accuracy and Precision in Measurements

items.item_title: What is the distinction between measurement trueness and precision?
items.text: The terms trueness and precision refer to different aspects of measurement. According to the International Organisation for Standardisation (ISO), "trueness" is defined as the closeness of agreement between the arithmetic mean of a large number of test results and the true or accepted reference value. "Precision," in contrast, is defined as the closeness of agreement found between different, independent test results. Precision, therefore, relates to the variability observed between repeated measurements. In statistics, a lack of trueness is commonly referred to as "bias". A lack of precision is described as "variability". It is noted that tests performed on identical materials under identical circumstances generally do not produce identical results. This is because the factors that influence the measurement's outcome cannot all be completely controlled.

items.item_title: How is the term "accuracy" defined by different standards?
items.text: The word "accuracy" is used in related but slightly different ways. The International Organisation for Standardisation (ISO) standard 5725 uses "accuracy" to describe the combination of both "trueness" and "precision". In this context, accuracy is the overall term. Conversely, the Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM) defines accuracy as the closeness of agreement between a single measured quantity value and a true quantity value of the item being measured. Dictionaries provide similar technical definitions, describing accuracy as the degree to which a measurement conforms to a standard or a correct value. The BIPM suggests that care must be taken to explain which sense of the term is being used. Sometimes it refers to a single measurement, while in other cases it refers to a set of measurements where both trueness and precision are assumed to be good.

items.item_title: What is measurement "bias"?
items.text: In statistics, the term "bias" is used to refer to a lack of trueness. Bias is formally defined as the difference between an estimator’s expected value and the true value of the parameter that is being estimated. In some experimental setups, external factors can change the measured value, which introduces a bias. This bias is calculated as the difference between the mean of the measurements and the reference value. While there is no single established methodology for assigning a numerical value to accuracy, bias can be managed. It is generally intended that calibration procedures for a measuring instrument should focus on establishing and correcting this bias. The systematic part of any measurement error is usually related to the trueness, or bias, of the measurement.

items.item_title: What is the difference between repeatability and reproducibility?
items.text: Both repeatability and reproducibility are concepts included within the definition of precision. They are used to describe the variability of a measurement method. Reproducibility is defined as the variation in a measurement process that occurs when it is performed under different conditions. These differing conditions include different instruments, operators, environments, or time periods. Reproducibility is said to incorporate more factors of variability than repeatability. Repeatability, by contrast, refers to the variation that arises even when efforts are made to keep all conditions constant. This means the measurement is performed with the same instrument and operator, in the same environment, and within a short time period.

items.item_title: How are measurement errors and precision quantified?
items.text: Measurement precision, or more specifically imprecision, is quantified numerically. It is expressed using the standard deviation, the variance, or the coefficient of variation. A related concept is measurement error, also called observational error. This is defined as the difference between the "true" value and the measured value. This error is composed of two parts. The systematic part of the error is related to the measurement's trueness, or bias. The random part of the error is linked to the measurement's precision. All measurement procedures have unavoidable random errors. If a measured value's deviation from a reference value is within the scope of these random errors, a true deviation cannot be established.

page_title: Liquid-liquid Phase Separation In Biology

items.item_title: What are membrane-less cellular compartments?
items.text: The organization of space inside cells is important for cell function. Cellular compartmentalization often involves organelles separated by a membrane, such as mitochondria, which ensure regions of specialized functions. Additionally, other cell compartments exist that are delineated without membranes. These compartments, defined as biomolecular or cellular condensates, retain specialized functions. They are characterized by a liquid-like state. In these states, components contained inside the liquid can easily move and mix. Transport in and out occurs through fast diffusion, yet neighbour compartments stay separated. Examples of these cellular liquid states include P bodies, which are involved in RNA storage, and the nucleolus, which produces ribosomes.

items.item_title: What is liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS) in biology?
items.text: Inside the cell, different liquid-phase compartments must remain separated. Liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS) is the phenomenon that regulates this cell compartmentalization. LLPS occurs because of a different molecular composition between the compartments. A local concentration of specific molecules in one compartment keeps it separate. The boundaries that form between these compartments allow the passage of certain molecules but not others. This local concentration can be generated by the spontaneous nucleation of a given molecule. In vitro experiments have shown that a minimal concentration is required before a molecule will nucleate and undergo phase separation. For example, the centrosome is generated by the nucleation of molecules around a centriole.

items.item_title: What types of molecules are associated with phase separation?
items.text: Several molecular features are associated with phase separation. Proteins with multiple binding sites, known as multivalency, can induce phase separation. This allows for multiple intermolecular interactions. These interactions generate rapid phase transitions that are visible inside the cell as liquid droplets. Multi-domain proteins, including those with non-soluble domains, are also associated with phase separation. IDPs (intrinsically disordered proteins) are another group associated with this phenomenon, owing to their amino-acid composition and conformational states. RNP (ribonucleoproteins) granules are also involved and can switch from liquid to solid phases. The FUS protein, for example, is an RNA binding protein that can be nuclear or accumulate in cytoplasmic liquid-phase stress granules.

items.item_title: How is LLPS regulated and what happens when it is de-regulated?
items.text: LLPS is a regulated process. Liquid-like cellular compartments can grow, fuse with other compartments (a process called droplet fusion), exchange molecules, or turn into more solid states during droplet maturation. Cells can regulate phase separation by controlling the concentration of proteins. This can be done, for instance, by the regulation of transcription or by changing the protein's cellular localization. Post-translational modifications are also used for regulation, as they can affect the solubility of proteins. De-regulation of these liquid-liquid phase transitions can lead to disease. Aberrant aggregation of molecules can cause the loss of the liquid state of a compartment. This protein aggregation is a sign of neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's or Parkinson's disease. De-regulated FUS aggregates, for example, are linked to amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).

page_title: Temperature Control Of S. Pombe Cytokinesis

items.item_title: Why is S. pombe (fission yeast) used as a model organism to study cytokinesis?
items.text: Fission yeast is considered a model of choice for studying cytokinesis. This is partly due to the availability of temperature-sensitive mutants. The existence of fluorescently tagged proteins for this organism is another factor. Additionally, S. pombe spends a large proportion of its cell division time in the cytokinesis phase, which makes it easier to observe. The formation and positioning of the contractile ring have been extensively studied in this organism. Researchers have focused on deciphering the positioning, assembly, maintenance, and constriction of this ring, which leads to the separation of the two daughter cells. The use of fluorescent-tagged proteins combined with high-resolution imaging allows for very precise analyses of these events.

items.item_title: What is the general process of cytokinesis?
items.text: Cytokinesis is a cellular process in which the cytoplasm and chromosomes of a single cell are divided. This division process generates two daughter cells. The mechanism is well conserved through evolution. It requires an interaction between myosin motor II proteins and actin filaments. These components come together to form a contractile ring. This ring is built at the site of cell division. It will then constrict the cell, leading to the formation of the two daughter cells. After the ring has totally constricted and disassembled, a septum starts to form. The formation of this septum leads to the final fission of the plasma membrane.

items.item_title: How is the contractile ring positioned in S. pombe?
items.text: The mid1p protein is a main actor in positioning the contractile ring. During interphase, mid1p is located in structures that are called nodes. These nodes, found at the cortico-medial area of the cell, exchange positional cues with the cell nucleus. The final position of the ring is governed by both permissive and restrictive positional information. There are also inhibiting mechanisms that prevent the ring from forming at the cell tip. For example, the pomp1p kinase is a protein that acts to exclude mid1p from localizing at the cell end tip. This system ensures the ring is formed in the correct medial location for proper cell division.

items.item_title: What occurs during the maturation of the contractile ring?
items.text: As the cell cycle progresses, the nodes maturate. This maturation involves the addition of nuclear and cytoplasmic Mid1p, myosin II, Rng2, F-BAR, and cdc12 proteins. At maturation, a formin protein, Cdc12, interacts with profilin to polymerize actin filaments. These newly formed filaments elongate along the plasma membrane and make contact with other nodes. After the actin filaments are formed, the nodes start to move and condense, which creates a continuous ring. This node movement is ensured by the interaction between the node's myosin II protein and the actin filaments. At this point, the actin filaments within the ring become contractile and begin to constrict the cell.

page_title: S. Pombe : Temperature Control Of Microtubules Assembly

items.item_title: Why is S. pombe used for studying microtubules?
items.text: The organism S. pombe is applied in studies of microtubule assembly. Fission yeast is noted for its ease of manipulation. It also has a short dividing time. Additionally, temperature-sensitive mutant strains of this yeast exist. These characteristics permit researchers to examine the function of microtubules in different aspects of S. pombe biology. The assembly of microtubules is known to be a temperature-controlled factor. The function of microtubules in S. pombe cell division, growth, and polarity has been documented. The availability of temperature-sensitive mutants is particularly relevant for these types of investigations. This allows for experiments, such as temperature-shift experiments, to be conducted to understand cellular processes.

items.item_title: What is the structure of a microtubule and how is it assembled?
items.text: A microtubule is a hollow cylinder and a polarized non-covalent polymer. It has a plus and a minus end. The structure is composed of 13 protofilaments. Each of these protofilaments is composed of alpha and beta-tubulin heterodimers. There are three phases in microtubule assembly. The first is the nucleation phase, which involves the formation of the alpha and beta-tubulin dimers. In S. pombe, microtubule nucleation happens in perinuclear microtubules organizing centers, which are called spindle pole bodies. The second phase is elongation, a growing phase during which tubulin dimers are added to the microtubule plus end. This addition allows microtubules to elongate. Finally, an equilibration phase is reached when the concentration of polymerized material is equivalent to the free material in the cytoplasm.

items.item_title: How is microtubule stability affected by temperature?
items.text: Microtubules are structures that change, with a constant addition and removal of tubulin dimers at the microtubule plus end tip. When tubulin dimers depolymerize and microtubules begin to shrink, this is referred to as "catastrophe". The switch from a catastrophe phase to a growing phase is called a "rescue" phase. Once microtubules are formed, their stability becomes dependent on temperature. Subjecting the cells to a low temperature, such as 6°C, is sufficient to obtain microtubule depolymerization. This also causes the relocation of microtubule-associated proteins to the perinuclear domain. This cold-shock effect is reversible, and microtubules can polymerize again with heat. An exception is noted in fishes from cold water, which possess tubulin that is cold resistant and can polymerize at very low temperatures (-1.8°C).

items.item_title: What has been learned from temperature-shift experiments in S. pombe?
items.text: Temperature-shift experiments have been used with temperature-sensitive S. pombe mutants. One study used a series of cold and heat-sensitive beta-tubulin mutants. It was shown that while microtubule depolymerization triggers the spindle checkpoint, this checkpoint could be quickly bypassed with heat. After microtubule depolymerization, cytokinesis was delayed for two hours at 20°C. The delay was only 45 minutes at 25°C, and no notable delays were observed at 32°C. Following these findings, an observation was made that S. pombe can still undergo "unusual nuclear fission" even in the absence of spindle microtubules. Other studies have also used temperature-shift experiments to examine the function of microtubule-associated proteins and motor proteins in spindle elongation.

page_title: Use of temperature sensitive SV40 large T antigen for conditional cell line transformation

items.item_title: What is the SV40 large T antigen?
items.text: Simian Virus 40 (SV40) is a DNA virus from the Polyomaviridae family that can infect various vertebrate species and is capable of causing solid tumours. During its infection cycle, viral replication is maintained by SV40's own proteins along with the host cell's replication machinery. The SV40 large T antigen is a main regulator of this viral replication process. It is a hexameric DNA helicase that is transcribed early during the infection. In addition to its role in viral replication, the T antigen also functions to inactivate tumour suppressor proteins, such as p53 and Rb. This action induces the host cell to enter the S phase, which in turn promotes the replication of the virus.

items.item_title: How is the SV40 T antigen used in biological research?
items.text: The SV40 T antigen has been used in biological research for two primary applications. It is employed as an inducer for the immortalisation of cell lines. It is also used for the generation of transgenic mouse models designed to study human cancers. Both of these methods have reportedly contributed to the current understanding of cancer biology. The protein's function includes the inactivation of tumour suppressor proteins like p53 and Rb. This inactivation induces the cell's entry into the S phase. This mechanism of promoting cell proliferation is harnessed by researchers for immortalising cells in culture or for generating cancer models in animals.

items.item_title: What is a temperature-sensitive SV40 large T antigen?
items.text: A temperature-sensitive (ts) mutant is a specific version of the SV40 large T antigen protein. The tsA58 variant is one such mutant that has been used as a tool for conditional cell line transformation. The use of ts mutants, such as those described by Brugge and Butel, allowed for an initial understanding of the T antigen protein's function in cell transformation. Additional temperature-sensitive mutations of the protein have also been described since then. This type of mutant protein allows its activity to be controlled by the environment. The protein is active at one temperature but becomes inactive when the temperature is changed to a different, non-permissive level.

items.item_title: How does conditional transformation with the tsA58 mutant work?
items.text: Conditional cell line transformation uses temperature-sensitive mutants like tsA58. Cell lines that have been transformed with this mutant will proliferate when they are maintained at the permissive temperature, which is 33°C. However, these same cells show proliferation arrest when they are shifted to the restrictive temperature of 39°C. This cessation of proliferation occurs because the T antigen protein is inactivated at the higher, restrictive temperature. This method has been used, for example, to induce senescence in fibroblasts in a temperature-regulated manner. It has also been applied to generate conditionally immortalised epithelial cell lines from transgenic mice and to create cell lines for blood-brain barrier models.

items.item_title: What are the benefits of using temperature shifts for protein inactivation?
items.text: The use of temperature-sensitive mutant proteins is a method for achieving conditional inactivation of proteins in multiple model organisms and cell lines. This approach has certain benefits compared to inactivation with chemicals. Temperature shifts are described as efficient. They are also considered easy to perform in a laboratory setting. Furthermore, this method is noted to have minor off-target effects. This is different from chemical inactivation methods, which may introduce other unintended effects within the cells. These attributes make temperature control a useful technique for researchers studying protein function.

page_title: Thermophile And Cryophile Archaea And Bacteria

items.item_title: What are thermophiles and cryophiles?
