items.text: Extremophilic organisms are defined by their capability for growth in extreme environments. Organisms that are adapted to high temperatures, up to 122°C, are called thermophiles. In contrast, organisms that live at low temperatures, down to \-20°C, are known as cryophiles or psychrophiles. Some of these organisms are obligate thermophiles, meaning they thrive at these temperatures. Others are described as thermotolerant, and they exhibit suboptimal growth in these conditions. Extreme hot and cold environments are ubiquitous on Earth. While these habitats are incompatible with mesophile organisms, which grow optimally between 20°C and 45°C, extremophile species have been identified in all three domains of life.

items.item_title: How do thermophilic archaea and bacteria adapt their membranes to heat?
items.text: One example of evolutionary thermal adaptation is the modification of membrane lipid composition. The stability and permeability of the bilayer membrane must be maintained at high temperatures. This is achieved by modifying the percentages of different lipids. A primary difference between archaea and bacteria is their membrane lipid composition. This distinction means their mechanisms for temperature adaptation are also different. Thermophile archaea, for example, keep their membrane functional across the whole range of temperatures by using their unique isoprenoid chains. Thermophilic bacteria, however, show modifications in their fatty acid composition based on the surrounding temperature.

items.item_title: What other adaptation mechanisms do these organisms use?
items.text: In addition to membrane lipid changes, other examples of thermal adaptation are noted. These include the amino acid composition of proteins, which leads to their thermal stability. The number of ORFs encoding for heat shock proteins and chaperones is another mechanism. Specialized DNA repair systems and variations in genome size are also listed as adaptations. Furthermore, horizontal gene transfer has been shown to be a consequential factor in the evolutionary adaptation of thermophiles. This process provides an additional level of genome plasticity. Horizontal gene transfer can occur between species that belong to different domains.

items.item_title: Why are these extremophiles useful in biotechnology?
items.text: Thermophile archaea and bacteria are the source of valuable tools for molecular biology, such as the Taq DNA polymerase used for PCR. The understanding and use of these species, with their unique metabolic and enzymatic activities, is central to the development of multiple industrial applications. Industrial processes with a reduced environmental impact are receiving increasing attention for sustainable growth. In this area, cryophiles are used as a source of cold-active enzymes and biosurfactants. These can be produced in a cold, low-impact manner. They may replace chemical surfactants in the food industry, detergent production, or the pharmaceutical industry. Thermophiles are also valued by industry. The archaea Sulfobolus, for instance, is used as a source of enzymes and metabolites in the food, textile, and pharmaceutical industries because of their thermal stability.

page_title: Heat Shock Protein Hsp70

items.item_title: What are heat shock proteins (Hsp) and what is their general function?
items.text: Heat shock proteins (Hsp) were first identified as chaperones. They had a function in protein folding in response to stress. Bacteria can sense environmental changes and respond to them to maintain cell homeostasis, and Hsps are main elements in this cellular response. However, multiple functions in non-stress conditions have also been described. These functions include the maintenance of protein conformation. They also help with the regulation of intracellular protein localization. Another function is the avoidance of protein aggregation. Different types of Hsp have been found. They have been named according to their molecular weight.

items.item_title: How does the Hsp70 chaperone system function in bacteria?
items.text: Hsp70 chaperones require functional partners to work; they do not work alone. These partners are the co-chaperones J protein and the nucleotide exchange folder-NEF protein. In bacteria, the Hsp70 chaperone is DnaK. It has an important part in protein refolding after stress. DnaK works with its partners, which are GrpE (a nucleotide exchange factor) and DnaJ (a J protein). The DnaK protein also contains an ATPase N-terminal domain. In addition to this system, other chaperone systems that are composed of homologues to these three proteins have also been described in bacteria.

items.item_title: How do Hsp proteins relate to temperature changes in bacteria?
items.text: Temperature is an important abiotic stress that bacteria can face. Bacteria are adapted to grow at a certain range of temperatures. They trigger heat shock stress responses when exposed to heat and cold. The components of the Hsp machinery are able to work as thermal sensors. This capability allows for a switch in function depending on the temperature. Because of this, thermal adaptation allows bacteria to grow at non-optimal temperatures. Furthermore, thermophile and cryophile bacteria are adapted to live in extreme temperatures. Studies of Hsp70 (DnaK) in thermophile bacteria have shown that different structural modifications are part of this thermal adaptation.

items.item_title: Why is Hsp70 considered a target for drug discovery?
items.text: Hsp70 has an important function in protein and cell homeostasis. It is not surprising that these genes have been linked to multiple diseases, ranging from cancer to neurodegenerative diseases. A loss of function in the Hsp70 pathway can lead to protein misfolding and protein aggregation. These two conditions are known as hallmarks of Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases. For this reason, the potential interest in targeting Hsp70 proteins for drug discovery is considered very large. Applications for such drugs could range from anti-bacterial uses to targeting human diseases.

page_title: Drosophila Embryo Live Cell Imaging

items.item_title: What occurs during Drosophila embryo development?
items.text: Drosophila embryo development, or embryogenesis, is a quick process that is completed in 24 hours after egg fertilisation. It is followed by three larval stages. Early development happens in a syncytium. In this state, nuclear divisions and very rapid DNA replication occur, but cell divisions do not. After 6000 nuclei have migrated to the periphery of the syncytium, cellularisation begins. The syncytial blastoderm is then generated. Next, in a process called gastrulation, cell shapes are changed and cells migrate. The germ layers of the future body plan (mesoderm, endoderm, and ectoderm) are established by this action. The process of cell fate determination is directed by underlying patterned gene expression. This expression pattern is established at the blastoderm stage, prior to gastrulation.

items.item_title: What preparations and considerations are involved in mounting embryos for live imaging?
items.text: Live imaging of D. melanogaster embryos requires non-invasive mounting procedures. Several points must be considered, based on the biological process being studied. These points include the duration of the timelapse and whether gas permeability is needed. It is also necessary to determine if squeezing of the embryo is suitable. For instance, methods that require embryo squeezing would not be the most appropriate for morphogenesis studies. Dehydration, hypoxia, and tissue deformation are other important issues that must be taken into consideration. Before any mounting method is used, a prior step of dechorionation of the embryos is required. This is followed by placing the embryos in the desired orientation, such as dorsolateral or ventrolateral, depending on the specific experiment.

items.item_title: What is the purpose of halocarbon oil in live imaging?
items.text: The use of halocarbon oil is required by many mounting methods for Drosophila embryos. This substance serves several functions. Dehydration of the embryo is prevented by the halocarbon oil during the imaging process. The oil is also permeable to oxygen. Hypoxia in the tissues can be avoided during long imaging sessions due to this property. Additionally, the physical properties of the oil are useful for time-lapse imaging. Motion of the embryos is prevented by the oil. This ensures the embryo remains in place while images are acquired. For example, embryos in halocarbon oil are placed in a sandwich between a glass slide and coverslip. They are also used for mounting on a glass bottom Petri dish. In the hanging drop protocol, the embryo is also placed in a halocarbon oil drop.

items.item_title: What are some common mounting methods for fly embryos?
items.text: Several mounting methods are commonly used for the live imaging of fly embryos. One method involves mounting between a glass slide and a glass coverslip. For this technique, embryos in halocarbon oil are placed in a sandwich configuration. Spacers of a desired thickness, such as double-sided tape, can be used to create imaging chambers. In another method, a glass bottom Petri dish is used, and embryos are mounted on it with halocarbon oil. The addition of large volumes is possible with this method, which prevents evaporation and allows for longer imaging. Chambered coverslips provide similar advantages, allowing multiplexed experiments on a single glass coverslip. Permeable membranes made of materials like Teflon are also used to provide gas permeability for long imaging sessions without hypoxia risk.

page_title: Confocal microscopy : Principles and application

items.item_title: What is laser scanning confocal microscopy?
items.text: Laser scanning confocal microscopy is an established imaging technique. It is used to obtain two-dimensional or three-dimensional high-resolution images from samples that are relatively thick. The method is considered effective for imaging fluorescent samples. This effectiveness is due to its high efficiency and its ability to reduce background noise. A main feature of the technique is the capability to optically section samples. This allows for different focal planes to be imaged individually. These separate images can then be used to create 2D or 3D views. The resulting micrograph is cleared of signals that are out of focus.

items.item_title: How does confocal microscopy reduce background noise?
items.text: This technique was invented to avoid problems associated with wide-field microscopy. One of these problems is background noise that comes from different, out-of-focus planes. The main principle that overcomes this limitation is the use of pinholes. These pinholes are designed to select photons based on the focal plane from which they originate. Excitation light is concentrated into a spot on a specific focal plane. Photons emitted by nearby focal planes, which would normally create background noise, are physically stopped by the photomultiplier pinhole. As a result, these out-of-focus photons do not contribute to the final image formation.

items.item_title: How does this method help reduce photobleaching?
items.text: Fast photobleaching is a problem associated with traditional wide-field microscopy. In a conventional fluorescence microscope, the excitation light illuminates the entire area of interest at the same time. This continuous, broad exposure causes the loss of fluorescence over time to be much stronger. Confocal microscopy helps to lessen this effect. The method uses pinholes to concentrate the light. Fluorophores are excited only during the specific moment of spot illumination and acquisition. The sample is scanned by a small spot of light rather than being uniformly exposed. This reduces the overall time the fluorophores are excited, which in turn mitigates the photobleaching phenomenon.

items.item_title: What are the main drawbacks of this microscopy technique?
items.text: One of the main drawbacks is the scanning speed. The light is concentrated in a small spot, which must be moved at high speed to scan the entire sample. This process is slower than wide-field illumination. A second limitation is related to the confocal pinhole. While the pinhole's selectivity is good for reducing noise, it also greatly decreases the amount of light that goes to the sensor. Therefore, the sensor must be very sensitive, or the exposure time must be increased. Highly sensitive sensors, such as photomultipliers, can be prone to generating false signals, or noise. This noise is generally removed by repeating the scan, but this action further increases the total scanning time.

page_title: Agar pad for live cell imaging

items.item_title: Why are agar pads used for cell imaging?
items.text: Imaging non-adherent cells is difficult because they move. Living cells are motile, while fixed cells can move due to Brownian motion. It is necessary to prevent the movement of cells that are being imaged. This is especially needed when doing multichannel fluorescent microscopy, as the channels must be merged for colour overlays. Agar pads are used because they prevent both the motility of live cells and the Brownian motion of fixed cells. This makes them a suitable method for imaging multiple model organisms. These organisms include bacteria, yeast, worm embryos, adult worms, plants, and zebrafish embryos. They can also be combined with other methods, such as anaesthetics for C. elegans, to further improve immobilisation.

items.item_title: What is an agar pad made of?
items.text: Agar pads are thin, flat layers of an agarose matrix. Although they are commonly referred to as "agar" pads, they are usually made from agarose. Agar is a polysaccharide substance obtained from red algae. Agarose is the main component of agar and is obtained after the agar has been purified. As a polymer, agarose creates a porous matrix. This structure traps cells, or molecules for molecular biology applications, preventing them from moving. Nutrient and gas exchange can still occur through this matrix, which makes the method compatible with live imaging. No toxicity for cells has been described as a result of growing in agar.

items.item_title: Can the composition of agar pads be modified?
items.text: Yes, the percentage of agarose used to make the pads can be varied. Very standard percentages are from 0.5% to 2%. Higher percentages of agarose, up to 10%, have been used to immobilize adult worms. This method has the benefit of a lack of toxicity compared to other methods. Adult worms in these pads can be imaged for hours, provided the pad is properly sealed to avoid desiccation. It is also possible to replace agarose with other polysaccharides. For example, while 2% agar is standard for C. elegans embryo imaging, this can be replaced by 4% sucrose 0.1M NaCl for delicate early embryos or those sensitive to osmotic stress.

items.item_title: How are agar pads prepared for microscopy?
items.text: Agarose pads must be prepared so they are thin and flat. This allows them to be placed in a sandwich between a glass slide and a coverslip. The required thickness of the pad depends on the organism that will be imaged. One protocol shows how to prepare a 250µM thick pad using simple laboratory materials like glass slides and tape. The thickness of the tape is an important detail in this method. If the tape is too thin, it will result in very thin agar pads. These pads are often fragile and difficult to manipulate. This mounting protocol is also compatible with thicker pads, which may be needed for thicker specimens such as zebrafish embryos.

items.item_title: What are the main limitations of using agar pads?
items.text: There are several main limitations when using agar pads for live cell imaging. One limitation is the impossibility of changing the media, which prevents actions like applying drugs during the experiment. Another risk is the pad drying out, also known as desiccation. For imaging experiments that take a long time, the limiting nutrient availability within the pad can also be a problem. These issues must be considered when planning experiments. Microfluidic technologies are intended to address these specific limitations. These newer technologies aim to ensure a more controlled environment for the cells being studied.

page_title: Introduction to spinning disk confocal microscopy

items.item_title: What is spinning disk confocal microscopy used for?
items.text: Spinning disk confocal microscopy is a specific setup of confocal microscopy. It is capable of imaging at a much faster rate. For this reason, spinning disk confocal microscopy is found to be particularly useful when imaging living samples or recording fast phenomena. It has been observed to have less phototoxicity. It is also less prone to photobleach fluorophores when compared to laser scanner confocal microscopy. This is because photodamage is related more to intensity (dose/time) rather than the total quantity of photons. Illuminating spots multiple times with lower photon amounts, as spinning disk does, results in fewer secondary reactions. It is now considered a highly regarded method for observing samples with a small to medium thickness.

items.item_title: How does this method achieve faster imaging than laser scanned confocal microscopy (LSCM)?
items.text: The speed of spinning disk microscopy comes from how it illuminates the sample. In LSCM, a single laser spot must scan the entire sample. This scanning speed is physically limited by the response time of the galvanic mirrors. In contrast, spinning disk microscopes illuminate the sample with thousands of light spots at the same time. This simultaneous illumination covers the whole sample area in a much shorter time. This allows spinning disk systems to be much faster. They are able to obtain more than three hundred frames per second. In LSCM, acquiring good images can easily take up to one second per frame.

items.item_title: How is the image collected in a spinning disk microscope?
items.text: In spinning disk confocal microscopes, the returning signal is collected directly by CCD sensors. This is different from LSCM, which requires dedicated software to process signals to reconstruct the image. With the spinning disk method, the acquisition speed of the CCD can be set. This allows enough data to be collected to obtain the full focal plane image directly. No software algorithm is needed for this step. An advantage of using a CCD sensor instead of a photomultiplier is related to noise. Although CCD sensors are less sensitive, they are much less prone to noise.

items.item_title: What is the function of the Nipkov disks in this system?
items.text: The central technology of spinning disk microscopy consists of one or two Nipkov disks. In the common two-disk configuration, the disks have specific functions. The disk that is closer to the sample in the optical scheme has hundreds of pinholes. This disk is used to illuminate and collect light from a specific focal plane. The second disk also has hundreds of small holes, but each one hosts a micro-lens. These micro-lenses collect photons from the main laser beam. The photons are then collimated by the micro-lenses into the pinholes of the first disk. An electrical motor accelerates both disks to a high speed. This action generates the specific illumination pattern of spinning disk microscopes.

page_title: Introduction to two-photon excitation microscopy

items.item_title: What is two-photon excitation microscopy?
items.text: Two-photon excitation microscopy is a specific imaging technique. It is based on the capability, under certain conditions, to excite one electron from its ground state by using two photons. The light used to create the excitation has a longer wavelength than the fluorescent light that is collected. This is different from single-photon methods, where the excitation light has more energy than the emitted light. The principle was originally predicted by M. Goeppert-Mayer in 1931 and later demonstrated in 1961\. Because longer wavelengths are used, the method is considered cell-friendly. It allows for the imaging of living specimens with few, if any, physiological alterations.

items.item_title: How does two-photon absorption differ from single-photon absorption?
items.text: Most fluorescence microscopy relies on single-photon absorption. In this method, a fluorophore's electron is excited by a single photon that has more energy than the light emitted during fluorescence. For instance, UV excitation is used to collect blue fluorescence. In two-photon microscopy, the electron is excited by two photons that have a longer wavelength than the resulting fluorescence emission. An electron is moved from the ground level by a first photon; it must then absorb a second photon while in its virtual state to reach the fully excited state. This is a second-order process, making it more difficult to obtain at low light intensities. Two-photon absorption only prevails over single-photon absorption when the photons are sufficiently dense.

items.item_title: What are the main advantages of using two-photon microscopy?
items.text: The way this microscope illuminates a sample provides several benefits compared to confocal microscopy. The main advantage is the ability to produce fluorescence using longer wavelengths, such as far-red and infrared light. These wavelengths are less subject to light scattering. This property greatly enhances the microscope's capability to penetrate deeper into dense tissue. Furthermore, longer wavelengths are less toxic to cells. The probability of secondary photochemical reactions, which cause photobleaching, is also much lower than what is observed in confocal microscopy. This makes the technique suitable for imaging living specimens with minimal physiological alteration.

items.item_title: How does the design of a two-photon microscope differ from a confocal microscope?
items.text: The optical scheme of a two-photon microscope is similar to a standard laser scanner confocal microscope. Both technologies use a spotted light from a laser source and two galvanic mirrors to scan the sample. The main differences involve the illumination source and the lack of a sample pinhole. In confocal microscopy, a sample pinhole is required to filter fluorescence that originates away from the focal plane. This is not needed in two-photon microscopy. The physical principle dictates that fluorescence only occurs at the focal plane where photon density is high enough. This difference gives two-photon microscopy a better resolution in the z-axis. Additionally, confocal lasers use constant emission, while two-photon systems use pulsed lasers to concentrate photons in each pulse.

page_title: Drosophila Life Cycle And Fly Anatomy

items.item_title: What are the main stages of the Drosophila life cycle and how long does it take?
items.text: The complete life cycle of Drosophila, from egg fertilisation to the adult, takes approximately 10 days when maintained at 25°C. This organism is used in developmental biology research because it is a holometabolous insect. This means major morphological differences happen between the larva and the adult animal, a process known as metamorphosis. The life cycle is composed of several developmental stages. These stages are embryogenesis, the larval stage, the pupal stage, and adult life. During these distinct phases, the structures and organs that make up the adult fly's anatomy are formed.

items.item_title: What happens during the embryogenesis stage of the fruit fly?
items.text: Embryogenesis is a fast process that is completed 24 hours after the oocyte is fertilised. A syncytial embryo develops rapidly from a single cell. Inside this early embryo syncytium, quick DNA replication and nuclear divisions take place. This activity generates up to 5000 nuclei per embryo. Nuclei then migrate to the periphery of the syncytium, and cellularization happens. This forms the syncytial blastoderm in a process called cleavage. Following this, during gastrulation, cells will change their shape and migrate. This action establishes the mesoderm, endoderm, and ectoderm, which are the layers for the future body plan.

items.item_title: What are the features of the larval and pupal stages in Drosophila?
items.text: The larval stage consists of three instars, which last for about 4 days altogether. During larval growth, most cell types are already differentiated and functional. This allows many biological questions to be addressed at this stage. For example, larvae have been useful for neuronal studies, providing a simpler model with only 10,000 neurons compared to the adult's 250,000. The pupal stage begins after the 3rd instar larva is encapsulated and lasts around 4 days. In this stage, many larval structures are lysed. New structures are formed from imaginal disks, which give rise to adult parts like the head, legs, and wings. Some structures, like the nervous system, are preserved.

items.item_title: What are some characteristics of the adult Drosophila?
items.text: The adult fly emerges from the pupal case through a process called eclosion. The lifespan of an adult is around 30 days, but this can be variable depending on the temperature. Drosophila is considered a good genetic model partly because a female can lay a high number of eggs, around 100 per day. This generates a large progeny after genetic crosses. The flies are also noted for having very easy growth requirements in laboratory conditions. The study of adult flies has resulted in notable findings in basic biological processes. Examples of these research areas include ageing, circadian clocks, and behavioural studies.

items.item_title: What is the function of the antennae in the adult fly?
items.text: The antennae are a pair of sensory elements located on the head part of the fly. Within the second antennal segment, there is a mass of receptor cells. This structure is called the Johnston organ. The Johnston organ is the hearing organ for the fruit fly. It permits the flies to sense different mechanosensory stimuli. These stimuli include sound, which is used during courtship, but also wind and gravity. Neuronal signalling from the antenna is sent to the brain. This signalling allows for behavioural responses to be made to these various stimuli.

page_title: Wide-field fluorescence microscopy (Fluorescent microscopy)

items.item_title: What is fluorescence microscopy?
items.text: Fluorescence is defined as a natural phenomenon. It begins with the absorption of light by a specific molecule known as a fluorophore. Almost immediately after this absorption, the molecule emits light. This emitted light possesses a longer wavelength than the absorbed light. This process has been widely exploited in many scientific fields, including microscopy. The technique of fluorescence microscopy is based on this capability. Samples are first labeled with specific fluorophores. The microscope system is then used to excite these fluorophores. Finally, the resulting emission of light from the sample is selectively collected by the instrument. The provided image, for example, shows actin filaments in an osteosarcoma cell line that were stained with phalloidin.

items.item_title: What is the physical process that causes fluorescence?
items.text: Fluorescence is the emission of electromagnetic radiation. This emission is produced when an excited electron decays from the singlet state (S1) back to its ground state (S0). First, a photon with specific energy (h\\𝜈) is absorbed. This absorption causes an orbital electron of the fluorophore to pass from the S0 energetic level to the S1 level. The energy of S1 is equal to S0 plus h\\𝜈. Electrons in the S1 state typically relax through non-radiative transitions. They eventually decay to the ground state by emitting electromagnetic radiation, which is usually visible light. The non-radiative phenomena (NRP) result in heat transfer. The fluorescence emission generates a photon with an energy equal to S0 \+ h\\𝜈 – NRPenergy. Therefore, the emitted photon has less energy and a longer wavelength. This is known as Stoke’s law.

items.item_title: What are phototoxicity and photobleaching?
items.text: A strong light source is needed to illuminate and excite the sample. This is because fluorescent emission is a statistically rare event. The light used is several orders of magnitude higher than the emitted fluorescence. This need for strong irradiation has drawbacks. Live specimens can suffer from photo-toxicity at high photon doses. This is especially true when shorter wavelengths are used. A second issue is photobleaching. High-intensity irradiation increases the probability of secondary photochemical reactions. These reactions are the main cause of emission decreasing over time. This loss of intensity is a known problem for some microscopy techniques. It has been demonstrated that the dose is more involved in photobleaching than the intensity alone. For instance, continuous milder irradiation might cause more fluorophore intensity loss than applying the same photon amount in intense pulses.

items.item_title: How are biological samples labeled for fluorescence microscopy?
items.text: Three main approaches are used to properly label biological samples. The first approach uses natural or synthetic molecules. These molecules show a particular selectivity for specific targets. The molecules are often naturally fluorescent, such as Dapi, or they are chemically modified by binding a fluorophore, like phalloidine. The second approach is used to visualize proteins of interest. This method is based on antibody selectivity. In the simplest case, an antibody against a specific protein is conjugated with a fluorophore. This is then used to visualize the presence and distribution of the target protein. The third approach relies on genetically engineered cells. These cells are modified to produce fluorescent proteins when certain genes are expressed.

page_title: Beyond the limits of light diffraction: super resolution microscopy

items.item_title: What is the diffraction limit in optical microscopy?
items.text: Light diffraction is a physical phenomenon that defines the resolution limits for optical microscopy. It was characterized by Abbe in 1873\. He found that the ability to resolve two points is related to the wavelength used for illumination. Due to this phenomenon, a microscope cannot resolve two objects if they are at a distance shorter than λ/2NA. The variable λ is the wavelength of light, and NA is the numerical aperture of the lens. This means objects with a lateral distance less than half the light's wavelength cannot be differentiated. This is a physical law, not a limit based on the quality of the optics. For optical microscopes, it is not possible to go beyond a resolution of 200nm using traditional methods.

items.item_title: How have scientists overcome the diffraction limit?
items.text: In the last decades, new light microscopy techniques have made it possible to go beyond the limits of diffraction. These developments are based on advances in fluorescent microscopy. They are also based on light source manipulation and image signal processing. These new methods are able to overcome the physical constraint of the Abbe law, which was first described in 1873\. Microscopes that use these new techniques are known as Super-Resolution microscopy. A comparison shows that what appears as a homogeneous and blurry signal in standard fluorescence microscopy can be turned into a detailed map of the signal when these newer methods are used.

items.item_title: What was Ernst Abbe's discovery regarding microscopy?
items.text: In 1873, the German physicist Ernst Abbe discovered how the resolving power of microscopes is limited by the diffraction of light. His research showed that the resolution of a microscope is not related to the quality of the microscopy components themselves. Instead, it is due to the wavelength of the light being used and the aperture of the optics setup. He found that the capability to resolve two points at a certain distance is related to the wavelength used to illuminate the object. Abbe also discovered that images are made of an array of diffraction-limited spots. These spots have different intensities and overlap one to another to produce the final image.

items.item_title: What is the basic principle of optical microscopy?
items.text: Optical microscopy, which is also referred to as light microscopy, is the oldest microscopy technique. The first basic light microscopes were developed during the 17th century. The principle is based on a light source of visible light, which is used to illuminate a sample. The light that passes through the sample is then collected. After collection, it is magnified by a group of lenses, specifically the objective and the ocular. This same principle is still used in the present day. This method is called bright field microscopy. It remains a useful technique for routine analysis and for fast screenings in both life and material science.

page_title: Fruit Fly Pupae

items.item_title: Why are Drosophila pupae used as a research model?
items.text: Drosophila pupae are used in research as a model to study developmental transitions, such as metamorphosis. The developmental transformations that happen from the 3rd instar larva until the adult fly is formed have been studied in the fruit fly pupae. This model is used because the pupae are easy to manipulate, dissect, and image. The pupal stage itself is a phase in the fly's life cycle that follows the encapsulation of the larva inside a puparium. This stage lasts approximately four or five days. This duration can depend on sex, with the stage being longer in males, as well as on circadian clocks and temperature.

items.item_title: What physical changes occur during the pupal stage?
items.text: During the pupal stage, many larval structures are lysed, or broken down. New structures are then formed from the imaginal disks. These imaginal disks are developed from larval undifferentiated cells. They eventually give rise to multiple adult structures. These structures include the adult head, legs, wings, thorax, and reproductive apparatus. While many larval structures are lysed, some are preserved during the pupal stage. Examples of preserved structures include the nervous system and the gonads. The adult fly finally emerges from the pupal case in a process called eclosion. Before this happens, the puparium turns black.

items.item_title: What directs the process of metamorphosis in the pupa?
items.text: Changes in gene expression are what propel the metamorphosis process. A central function is performed by the steroid hormone ecdysone, along with other hormones and neuropeptides. Ecdysone is initially synthesised in the larval prothoracic gland, and it also has a function in the moulting of larval stages. Later, pulses of ecdysone during the pupal stage permit imaginal disc differentiation. This process leads to the formation of adult structures through different transcription factors. Metamorphosis and the final eclosion are highly energy-demanding processes. Energetic rates are high in prepupal and late pupal steps, matching the metabolic activity of pupal tissues.

items.item_title: How are pupae prepared for observation in research?
items.text: Mounting protocols for whole-body imaging of pupae have been extensively used in developmental biology. Dissection of the pupa for the observation of live tissues is also a common method. There are examples of protocols for both dissecting and mounting pupae for live-cell imaging. A common first step in all these protocols is the removal of the opaque pupal case. Beyond this shared step, a general protocol for pupa dissection is available. Other, more specific protocols can also be found for particular areas of study. These include protocols for muscle observation, pupal eye discs, abdominal development, and observing pupal wings.

page_title: Live-cell and perfusion: weak protein-protein interactions

items.item_title: Why are weak protein-protein interactions studied in cells?
items.text: Weak protein-protein interactions are a common phenomenon found inside cells. These interactions are studied because they have a very important effect on cellular homeostasis and metabolism. This effect is particularly noted when main regulators are involved in the interactions. Examples of these regulators include AMPK, PDK1, TOR, or PGK. These specific examples are recognized as master regulators of cellular homeostasis, as they all control central metabolic pathways. Weakly bound protein complexes are understood to play a main part in metabolic, regulatory, and signaling pathways. This importance is partly due to the high tunability of their bound and unbound populations, which makes their study relevant. However, this same tunability makes weak binding (defined by micromolar to millimolar dissociation constants) difficult to quantify under biologically relevant conditions.

items.item_title: How was the interaction between GAPDH and PGK quantified inside the cell?
items.text: The interaction between GAPDH and PGK was examined by researchers (Sukenic, Ren, and Gruebele) in a 2017 paper. These two substances are sequential enzymes in the glycolysis catalytic cycle. Although they were known to interact weakly, the interaction had not been quantified in vivo. To study them, a method involving the rapid perturbation of cell volume was used. This method modulates the concentration of the weakly bound protein complexes. Cell volume was controlled by modulating the osmotic pressure of the media. A perfusion set-up was built by the authors, allowing them to switch between different media that varied in osmotic pressure. The resulting complex association and dissociation were then observed using FRET microscopy. This approach allows the detection of the dissociation constant and stoichiometry directly inside the cell.

items.item_title: What were the quantitative findings regarding the GAPDH and PGK complex?
items.text: The study focused on the specific case of GAPDH interaction with PGK. The experimental results were fitted to a quantitative model. This model produced specific findings for the complex. The dissociation constant was determined, with a resulting log Kd \= −9.7 ± 0.3. This quantification of the interaction had not previously been achieved in vivo. Furthermore, a prevalent stoichiometry of 2:1 for the GAPDH:PGK complex was identified by the model. By further characterizing the association of this complex, the authors were able to form suggestions about cellular regulation. These results demonstrate how the experimental method (cellular volume perturbation) can be used to obtain precise measurements for transient interactions in situ.

items.item_title: What broader suggestions arise from this research?
items.text: The research provides two main suggestions. First, the method of cellular volume perturbation is presented as a widely applicable tool. It can be used to detect transient protein interactions. It may also be used for other biomolecular interactions in situ. Second, a potential regulatory mechanism for cells is suggested. The authors proposed that cellular volume changes can be a regulatory mechanism that controls protein complex concentrations. An example of such a volume change occurs when cells enter into mitosis. The results suggest that cells could use this type of volume change to regulate their function. This regulation would be achieved by altering the concentrations of biomolecular complexes.

page_title: The Challenge for Development of Valuable Immuno-oncology Biomarkers

items.item_title: Why is the analysis of single biomarkers considered insufficient in immuno-oncology?
items.text: The measurement of individual biomarkers is generally not considered informative enough. This is because it often fails to adequately capture the intricate interactions within the immune tumour microenvironment (TME). The TME represents a unique aspect of the response to immuno-oncology agents. These interactions are not simple. New checkpoint modulator development, such as PD1/PDL-1 agonists and antagonists, makes this issue particularly apparent. Therefore, multiple potential biomarkers are likely required to gain a better understanding. Relying on one marker does not reflect the sophisticated relationship between the tumour and the innate immune system. Future investigations will likely need to combine several techniques.

items.item_title: What are the main objectives for developing better immuno-oncology biomarkers?
items.text: Immunotherapy development is an important breakthrough for cancer treatment. Antitumour efficacy has been observed in a wide variety of tumours. To improve the use of immunotherapy, new approaches must be developed. These approaches should help identify which patients are likely to achieve benefit from the treatment. Understanding the ideal choice and sequencing of the numerous available immuno-oncology agents for individual patients is necessary. This understanding helps to minimize therapeutic toxicities. It also helps manage costs. Real-time input from patients and their advocates during biomarker discovery is also required. This is to ensure that the investigations pursued will improve both clinical outcomes and quality of life.

items.item_title: What are some examples of the potential biomarkers currently under investigation?
items.text: Since multiple biomarkers are likely needed, several candidates are being studied. Current candidates in this area include PD-L1 expression. Another candidate is the presence of CD8+ tumour-infiltrating lymphocytes. Tumour mutation load and the related neoantigen burden are also being examined. Additionally, immune-related gene signatures are under investigation. Multiplex IHC assays represent another category of candidates. These specific assays are used to examine the pharmacodynamic and spatial interactions occurring within the TME. It is thought that the most fruitful investigations will use several of these techniques together. This combination approach helps predict response and interrogate mechanisms of resistance to therapy.

items.item_title: How should future immuno-oncology biomarker research be structured?
items.text: Research into immuno-oncology biomarkers must employ validated assays. This is a requirement for asking focused research questions. These investigations should use clinically annotated tissue collections. Furthermore, biomarker-focused clinical trial designs are needed to investigate specific endpoints. It is suggested that the most effective investigations will likely use several techniques together. This approach can be used to predict patient response and to interrogate mechanisms of resistance. One suggested path forward involves having access to technologies that can recreate the tumour microenvironment in vitro. Merging this capability with the patient's immune system and multiple endpoint analyses seems to be one way to address the current difficulties in biomarker identification.

page_title: Immuno-oncology Trial Endpoints: Capturing Meaningful Activity

items.item_title: Why are traditional paradigms for clinical research being reconsidered for immuno-oncology?
items.text: The field of immuno-oncology (I-O) has made it necessary to change the established paradigm for assessing toxicity and response in clinical research. The design and interpretation of cancer clinical trials have historically been guided by the conventional patterns of toxicity and efficacy. These patterns are the ones observed with chemotherapy and targeted agents. It has been found that these conventional patterns are not sufficient to fully inform the design of clinical trials for I-O. They also cannot properly guide therapeutic decisions in this specific field. Classical dose-response patterns, which are used in toxicity and response assessment, are often not applicable in immuno-oncology. This has led to a discussion about new possible endpoints and methods to get clinically relevant data.

items.item_title: How do responses to immune-targeted agents differ from conventional therapies?
items.text: Responses to agents that are targeted to the immune system are different from conventional chemotherapy. These I-O agents are noted to follow non-linear kinetics for both dose-response and dose-toxicity. This is a key difference from the patterns seen with chemotherapy and other targeted agents. These conventional patterns have historically driven the design of clinical trials. The non-linear nature of immune-targeted agents makes these older assessment methods insufficient. This mandates that new criteria for evaluating responses must be developed. These new evaluation criteria are needed to properly assess the activity of I-O therapies.

items.item_title: What new endpoints could be used in early-phase immuno-oncology trials?
items.text: For the early-phase clinical development of I-O therapies, different endpoints are being considered. It is suggested that surrogate endpoints driven by biomarkers may be a better option. These types of endpoints might be able to better capture the mechanism of action of the I-O agents. They could also provide a more accurate measure of the biological response to the treatment. It is proposed that these biomarker-driven surrogate endpoints could be incorporated prospectively into the design of early-phase I-O clinical trials. This is part of an effort to find new ways to obtain clinically relevant data, which is needed as new checkpoint modulators are developed.

items.item_title: What is the standard for late-phase trials and what other endpoints are being explored?
items.text: In late-phase clinical trials, overall survival is still considered the gold standard. This endpoint is used for the evaluation of the clinical efficacy of immuno-oncology (I-O) agents. However, the exploration of potential new surrogate endpoints is also encouraged. Examples of these novel surrogate endpoints include the objective response rate. Milestone survival is another endpoint being explored. In addition to these measures, it is also suggested that patient-reported outcomes should be assessed. Including these outcomes could help to redefine the endpoints for I-O clinical trials. This, in turn, could help to drive a more efficient process for drug development.

page_title: Microtubule Dynamics And MAPs

items.item_title: What are microtubules and what is their function within a cell?
items.text: Microtubules are polymers of tubulin. They are involved in many cellular processes. These processes include the formation of the mitotic spindle, cell transport, and cytokinesis. Cell motility, cell shape, and morphogenesis are also processes they are involved in. Microtubules are polymers that exist in an equilibrium state with their subunits, which are $\\alpha$ and $\\beta$-tubulin. These subunits form dimers and are the basic building blocks of the microtubules. The dynamics of microtubule assembly and disassembly are central to their cellular function. These functions are necessary for mitosis, cell motility, and many other processes. Their polymerization is a regulated process.

items.item_title: What are microtubule organizing centers and how are they structured?
items.text: Microtubules are nucleated from tubulin subunits at specific locations within the cell. These locations are mainly the centrosomes, also known as MTOCs (microtubule organizing centers). In fungi, the equivalent structure is the Spindle Pole Body (SPB). Centrosomes are composed of a pair of centrioles. These centrioles are comprised of nine microtubules that are organized in cylinders. The centrioles are surrounded by a dense pericentriolar material, or PCM. This material is associated with a high density of tubulin. While centrosomes are the main nucleating centers, centrosome-lacking centers do exist in nature. Cells can also divide without centrosomes, but this can lead to mitotic defects.

items.item_title: What are Microtubule Associated Proteins (MAPs) and what role do they play?
items.text: Microtubule Associated Proteins, or MAPs, are proteins that bind to microtubules. They are mostly structural proteins. Upon nucleation, microtubules are stabilized by their association with MAPs. MAPs have been isolated and identified from microtubules that were purified in laboratories from many tissues and species. In addition to their structural function, MAPs also have a role in other cellular processes. They are involved in the interactions of microtubules with other elements in the cell. One example of this is their interaction with vesicles during processes like cellular transport.

items.item_title: How are the dynamic changes in microtubules regulated?
items.text: Microtubule dynamics are highly regulated. This regulation occurs at their plus-end, which is where growth and shrinkage happen. These processes are known as rescue and catastrophe, respectively. They are actively regulated and respond to signalling and external cues. Plus-end binding factors, such as EB1, are important regulators of this growth. EB1 binds directly to the plus-end and recruits other regulatory factors. Dynamics are also modulated by post-translational modifications (PTMs). A wide range of modifications, including phosphorylation and acetylation, decorate the microtubule arrays. Other factors like calcium concentrations, certain drugs, and temperature are also important for microtubule dynamics.

page_title: Checkpoint modulation – A new way to direct the immune system against renal cell carcinoma

items.item_title: What were the limitations of older cytokine treatments for renal cell carcinoma?
items.text: Patients treated for metastatic renal cell carcinoma (mRCC) with cytokine treatments, such as Il-2 and IFN-$\\alpha$, experienced some positive outcomes. Disease stabilization or remission was seen in up to 30% of these patients. This effect, however, was a limited therapy because it only lasted for some months. The treatment's effectiveness was restricted due to an increase in regulatory T cells, or Tregs. It also caused a decrease in circulating myeloid and plasmacytoid dendritic cells. While newer targeted therapies improved survival, the prognosis remained limited, creating a need for treatments that provoke long-term responses.

items.item_title: What new therapeutic approach is being studied for mRCC?
items.text: A new way to treat renal cell carcinoma is being investigated. This method involves targeting immune checkpoint modulation. The approach developed from a deeper understanding of the immunology of T cell activation. These therapies use checkpoint inhibitors, which are mainly monoclonal antibodies. The function of these inhibitors is to enhance the co-stimulatory signals that are necessary for T cell activation, also known as priming. This strategy of T-cell re-activation holds the promise of directly affecting the tumour cell. It may have the ability to potentially cure a subset of patients with mRCC.

items.item_title: What specific checkpoint inhibitors are being investigated for renal cell carcinoma?
items.text: The new approach for mRCC treatment involves targeting specific immune checkpoint pathways. Research is focused on checkpoint inhibitors that act on these pathways. A review of the topic discusses the available clinical data and ongoing studies for the inhibition of two main axes. The first is the PD-1 (CD279) axis. The second is the CTLA-4 (CD152) axis. Therapies that block these pathways are intended to enhance T cell activation, allowing the immune system to direct an attack against the tumour. Potential future immunological targets are also being discussed in addition to these.

items.item_title: Why are new therapies for mRCC considered urgently warranted?
items.text: While targeted therapies like TKI and mTOR inhibitors have improved patient survival, the prognosis for mRCC remains limited. Therapies with a new mode of action are urgently needed. Specifically, there is a need for treatments that would provoke long-term responders. The goal is to achieve long-lasting complete remissions. This type of durable response was sometimes observed with older unspecific immunotherapy using cytokines. The aim is to find a new approach that can reliably produce these long-term results. Immunological checkpoint inhibition is being studied as one such approach that holds this promise.

page_title: Immune Checkpoint Modulators: An Emerging Antiglioma Armamentarium

items.item_title: What are immune checkpoints and how do tumours use them?
items.text: Immune checkpoints are a component of the immune system. They have become a focus of cancer therapies because their inhibition can be used to stimulate T cell activity against a tumour. Tumour cells are known to exploit these checkpoints. This exploitation is done to reduce the activation of the immune system. It also helps the tumour cells decrease antigen recognition. When tumours use these checkpoints, they can achieve immune evasion. This mechanism helps the tumour to avoid being attacked by the body's immune cells. Both local and systemic immune suppression can be a result of this tumour activity. By inhibiting these checkpoints, this tumour-induced suppression can be prevented.

items.item_title: What is the therapeutic strategy involving immune checkpoint modulators?
items.text: A therapeutic strategy has been developed based on immune checkpoints. It is considered a promising approach. The main goal is the stimulation of T cell activity against the tumour. The strategy is described as non-specific. It consists of a generalised activation of the immune system. This approach uses checkpoint inhibition. This inhibition is able to prevent the tumour from evading the immune system. It also works to prevent local and systemic immune suppression caused by the tumour. The therapy often uses monoclonal antibodies. These antibodies are used to target the checkpoint inhibitors. This creates a therapeutic blockade, allowing the immune system to become more active against the cancer.

items.item_title: What are some examples of checkpoint modulator therapies?
items.text: Monoclonal antibodies are one method used to target checkpoint inhibitors. This action results in a therapeutic blockade. Two examples of these antibodies are already in Phase 4 of clinical development. The first is anti-CTLA-4, which is also known as ipilimumab. The second is anti-PD-1, or nivolumab. In addition to these, many other targets are also being studied. These other targets are currently in earlier phases of development, such as Phase 1 or 2\. Examples of these targets include LAG-3, TIM-3, KIR, and GITR.

items.item_title: How are these therapies applied to gliomas and can they be combined with other treatments?
items.text: Immune checkpoint modulators are being examined as a therapy for gliomas. The role of immune checkpoints in gliomagenesis, which is the formation of gliomas, has been studied. Preclinical and clinical data are being reviewed. This information concerns the antitumour efficacy and clinical applications for checkpoints, specifically in relation to glioblastoma (GBM). This therapeutic strategy is not always used on its own. It is noted that the generalised activation of the immune system can be combined with other treatments. These other treatments include both chemotherapy and radiation therapy.

page_title: Liver function and liver on a chip

items.item_title: What are the primary functions of the liver?
items.text: The liver is a large internal organ that is central to maintaining homeostasis and metabolic functionality. Hepatocytes are the main liver cells and are responsible for over 500 functions. These include the metabolization of macromolecules like fats, proteins, and carbohydrates. Protein synthesis is also performed by these cells. The organ is responsible for the detoxification of the blood. It processes both endogenous substrates, such as bilirubin, and exogenous ones, like drugs. Glycogen and lipids are stored by hepatocytes. Additionally, hepatocytes produce bile. Bile is used by the body for the digestion and absorption of fats.

items.item_title: What are liver sinusoidal endothelial cells and fenestrations?
items.text: Liver sinusoids are capillaries that are composed of particular endothelial cells, which are known as liver sinusoidal endothelial cells (LSECs). These cells are considered a scavenger system that clears waste products from the blood. They also function as a selective filter for substances passing between the sinusoid and the hepatocytes. LSECs have a high endocytotic activity. A key characteristic is the presence of nanopores on their surface, as they do not have a basal lamina. These nanopores, or fenestrations, have a diameter of 150 to 175 nm. They are grouped together to form sieve plates, which permit molecules like drugs and proteins to pass through. LSECs are involved in chronic liver diseases and regeneration.

items.item_title: Why are standard 2D culture methods not sufficient for liver cells?
items.text: Standard 2D cell cultures are not able to reproduce the liver's physiological environment. The complex structure of the organ is not present in these simplified models. Specific cells, such as LSECs and hepatocytes, are known to be difficult to grow in standard 2D cultures. A major problem is that the cells often dedifferentiate early in the culture process. When this dedifferentiation occurs, the cells lose their characteristic functionalities. This makes 2D monocultures poor models for the liver's function. New approaches, such as liver-on-a-chip devices and 3D liver models, are being developed to simulate human liver physiology by copying its microstructure.

items.item_title: What is a "liver-on-a-chip" device?
items.text: An organ-on-a-chip is a bio-engineered system. It is designed to reproduce the key features of the fundamental unit of a human organ. When the liver is the organ of interest, it is called a liver-on-a-chip. These devices often share common characteristics. They are typically constructed from a biocompatible and transparent material, which permits visualization. The devices are also constructed with geometries that simplify the native tissue compartments. Perfusion systems are used to sustain the cells and recapitulate physiological conditions. These systems usually consist of pumps and tubing to regulate flow. Monitoring is often achieved through integrated sensors and imaging systems.

items.item_title: What are the main types of liver-on-a-chip systems?
items.text: Liver-on-a-chip systems can be broadly distinguished based on their main action, though combinations of these systems exist. The first type is cell aggregation systems, which are also known as in vitro liver models. These systems primarily offer the possibility to co-culture different liver cell types. Co-culturing is a fundamental requirement to support long-term culture and avoid cell dedifferentiation. This can be done using multiwell platforms with micropatterned ECM or by producing liver spheroids. The second type is architecture-mimicking systems. These systems simulate specific liver structures, like the lobule, to copy the native tissue and its chemical-physical stimuli. They use microfluidic platforms to create chambers where cells are perfused with medium.

page_title: Nanoscopy to improve drug screening and liver fenestrations

items.item_title: Why do many new drugs fail during development, and how is this related to the liver?
items.text: A large number of new medicines do not pass human clinical trials. It is estimated that 90% of compounds that pass preclinical screening will fail in human tests. A major reason for this situation is the low predictive power of the preclinical tests. Drug-induced liver injury (DILI) is responsible for 40% of these failures. The cell and animal models used in preclinical trials are not fully representative. 2D cell monocultures, while easy to manage, cannot show the function of a difficult organ such as the liver. Animal testing is not precise owing to the phylogenetic distance between species, which can mean different metabolic pathways. This situation might be mitigated by introducing new systems that better simulate liver function, such as laboratory-based liver models.

items.item_title: What are liver fenestrations and what is their function in the organ?
items.text: The liver contains capillaries called sinusoids, which are formed by liver sinusoidal endothelial cells (LSECs). These LSECs have nanopores on their surface. These nanopores are known as fenestrations. They have a diameter of 150 to 175 nm and are grouped into sieve plates. Fenestrations serve as a selective filter. They enable the passage of many types of molecules, including metabolites, drugs, and viruses, from the sinusoid to the hepatocytes. Fenestrations are not static structures; their number can change in response to stimuli like drugs, toxins, or diseases. LSECs are involved in many liver processes, such as chronic diseases and regeneration. Although their importance is known, many aspects, such as how they originate or what determines their size, are still not understood.

items.item_title: What is nanoscopy and why is it required for studying liver fenestrations?
items.text: Nanoscopy, also known as super-resolution microscopy, is a term for techniques that allow detection of very small structures. These structures are smaller than the diffraction limit of light, which makes them impossible to see with standard optical microscopy. Liver nanopores, or fenestrations, have a mean diameter of 150 to 175 nm. This small size is why they cannot be imaged with normal light-based methods. Previously, electronic microscopy (EM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) were used. These techniques, however, are technically demanding. They are also not fully compatible with investigating living cells, which is a decisive approach. Nanoscopy techniques developed more recently are suitable for studying live cells.

items.item_title: What are some of the different types of nanoscopy techniques?
items.text: Several nanoscopy methods have been developed that permit the visualization of liver nanopores. These systems also allow for live-cell applications and can provide multicolor images. Three techniques are Structured Illumination Microscopy (SIM), Single Molecule Localization Microscopy (SMLM), and Stimulated Emission Depletion (STED). Each one uses a different strategy. SIM can detect particles down to 100 nm by using a phase modulation of light through a moving grid. SMLM uses special fluorophores whose emissions are separated in time and space, allowing them to be distinguished even when very close. STED works by reducing the spot size of the detecting light beam. These nanoscopes are expected to be included in most biological laboratories in the next 10 years.

page_title: In Vitro Immune Organs-on-Chip for Drug Development

items.item_title: What is a major problem in the current drug development process?
items.text: For a drug to be approved by the FDA, it must pass a preclinical evaluation phase. This process relies on in vitro cell culture platforms and in vivo animal models before proceeding to clinical evaluation in human subjects. A high number of drugs fail during these clinical trials. This results in a very low ratio of 1-to-10,000 for a drug to gain approval and reach the market. This process is long and costly. A single drug development cycle can take up to 12 years and cost an average of 1.7 billion USD. This significant failure gap between preclinical and clinical studies may be due to the drug candidates' effects on the human immune system.

items.item_title: Why are existing preclinical models insufficient for testing immune responses?
items.text: The current drug development practice does not have reliable and sensitive techniques. These are needed to evaluate the immunotoxicity of drug candidates, which is their effect on the human immune system. This lack of good methods has contributed to the high failure rate for new drugs. Existing in vitro platforms are not able to emulate the complexity of the cellular microenvironment. They also cannot provide a relevant physiological model. Furthermore, animal models are far from being able to predict how human immune systems will respond to drugs. These limitations create a need for better testing systems.

items.item_title: What are "organs-on-chip" and how do they work?
items.text: "Organs-on-chip" is a technology that has emerged from recent advances in micro-engineering and biotechnology. These systems are in vitro devices. They are based on microfluidics. The technology is designed to emulate the physiology of tissues. It also aims to copy the complex biochemical microenvironment of cells. The important mechanical constraints that cells experience are also emulated. Organ-on-chip devices are tools that permit the study of human physiology. This study is done in controlled environments that simulate the conditions found in vivo.

items.item_title: What is the purpose of developing "immune system-on-chip" models?
items.text: There is a growing interest in developing "body-on-chip" devices to better predict the systemic effects of drug candidates. The goal of creating specific immune system models is to enhance the performance of new drugs during clinical trials. These models could help reduce the high attrition rate of drugs. They might also contribute to lowering the high costs associated with drug development. To achieve physiologically relevant immune responses, an advanced in vitro system would need to represent each of the main immune organs. These organs include the bone marrow, thymus, spleen, liver, lymph nodes, skin, and gut. These biomimetic immune models are being assessed for their relevance to drug discovery.

page_title: 3D cell culture and anticancer drug testing

items.item_title: Why are 2D cell cultures not sufficient for anticancer drug testing?
items.text: Monolayered 2D cell culture remains the most applied model for testing anticancer compounds. These models have been useful for understanding molecular pathways related to cell proliferation. A problem exists because 2D growth conditions do not include important components of the tumour microenvironment, or TME. The TME is a complex network that surrounds tumour cells. It includes vascular networks, the extracellular matrix, and various cell types. This TME is known to be involved in drug resistance. The simplified nature of 2D models is one reason why anticancer drug candidates often have poor outcomes when they are used in patients.

items.item_title: What is the tumour microenvironment and how does it cause drug resistance?
items.text: The tumour microenvironment (TME) is a complicated biochemical network of factors that surround tumour cells. The TME can affect gene expression, cell proliferation, and cell migration. The cytotoxicity of anticancer drugs is highly dependent on this environment. Several TME features cause drug resistance. The high cell density in solid tumours acts as a physical barrier. Changes in fluid pressure and ECM stiffness can also influence drug intake. Contacts between cancer cells and the ECM can activate signalling pathways. This induces resistance in a process called CAM-DR. Hypoxic conditions, or low oxygen, also increase the survival rates of cancer cells.

items.item_title: How do 3D models like spheroids and organoids represent a tumour?
items.text: Recent 3D culture technologies allow cancer models to be grown in more physiological conditions that represent the TME. Spheroids are one example. In this model, cells are densely packed. This structure is achieved through physical confinement. A gradient of oxygen concentration forms inside the spheroid. This creates an inner quiescent zone, similar to the conditions in a tumour mass. Increased drug resistance has been seen in spheroids. Organoids are another 3D model. These are generated from tissue-derived stem cells that can self-organize. Tumour organoids grown from patient tissue can show the morphology and behaviour of the original tumours.

items.item_title: What are "tumour-on-a-chip" models?
items.text: Organ-on-a-chip models are a further step in this field. These systems combine organotypic assemblies like spheroids or organoids with microfluidics technology. Microfluidics allows cells to be grown in a very controlled environment inside microdevices. Specific parameters, such as blood flow, nutrient gradients, and oxygen gradients, can be controlled. Shear stress can also be represented. This ability to recreate the TME has allowed "tumour-on-a-chip" models to be developed. A controlled environment can be maintained over time. This permits the monitoring of tumour dynamics. The assessment of drug efficacy and resistance over time is also possible.

page_title: Tumor microenvironment (TME)

items.item_title: What is the tumour microenvironment (TME)?
items.text: The tumour microenvironment (TME) is defined as the area surrounding tumour cells. It is a complicated biochemical network. This network is composed of several factors, including vascular networks, the extracellular matrix (ECM), and a mixed population of cells. This population includes cell types such as fibroblasts and immune cells. In living tissues, cells are known to respond to various cues from their local cellular surroundings. The TME is a stroma that has been highly modified. Within this environment, interactions between cells and the ECM, as well as released factors, are central to tumour development. Gene expression, cell proliferation, and cell migration can be affected by these interactions.

items.item_title: How are vascular networks formed in the TME?
items.text: The formation of a highly vascularized environment is known to assist tumour progression. This occurs because nutrients and other factors are provided to the tumour. The process of tumour angiogenesis (new vessel formation) is caused by the secretion of VEGF. VEGF, or Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor, is released by cancer cells, fibroblasts, and stromal immune cells. The resulting vascularization around the tumour is abnormal in its structure. Vessels are observed to be leaky. They also have occlusions and blind ends, which means they cannot properly oxygenate the neighbouring cells. This poor oxygen supply contributes to hypoxia within the tumour mass.

items.item_title: What is the function of the extracellular matrix (ECM) in the TME?
items.text: The extracellular matrix (ECM) is composed of various proteins and proteoglycans, such as collagen, laminin, and fibronectin. In tumour cells, this ECM is extensively remodeled. This remodeling is caused by factors released from different cell types in the TME. Contact between the cells and the ECM activates certain signalling pathways, including anti-apoptotic pathways. A form of drug resistance, termed CAM-DR, can also be induced by these interactions. The ECM contains many signalling factors. For example, Matrix Metallo Proteinases (MMPs) are secreted and function to hydrolyze ECM proteins. This modification of the ECM affects the ability of cancer cells to invade their surroundings.

items.item_title: What are carcinoma-associated fibroblasts (CAFs)?
items.text: Fibroblasts are the most numerous cell type found in the TME. They are sometimes even more numerous than the cancer cells. During tumour progression, these fibroblasts are modified. They are remodeled into a state known as CAFs, or carcinoma-associated fibroblasts. CAFs are functionally active. Normal fibroblasts are active during processes like wound healing, but CAFs are different. CAFs are constitutively active, meaning they are always on, and they do not undergo apoptosis. It is also noted that they cannot revert to a normal fibroblast state. The exact mechanisms that convert normal fibroblasts into CAFs are not completely understood. These activated CAFs have been shown to enhance metastasis.

items.item_title: What other cell types are found in the TME?
items.text: Several other cell types are present in the TME. Epithelial cells in the TME can experience the epithelial to mesenchymal transition (EMT). This is a process where cells lose their contacts. They also acquire motility and the capacity for invasion, which allows them to migrate from the original tumour site. Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) are also present. These progenitor cells are recruited to the TME and are changed into carcinoma-associated MSCs, which assist in tumour growth. Different types of immune cells, known as infiltrating immune cells (IICs), are also found. For example, macrophages are recruited to hypoxic zones and become activated as TAMs (Tumor-associated macrophages). TAMs release factors that contribute to tumour development.

page_title: Tumor Hypoxia and Metastasis

items.item_title: What is tumour hypoxia?
items.text: Solid tumours are noted for having areas of hypoxic tissue. These areas are distributed unevenly inside the tumour mass. Hypoxic growth is a defining feature of solid tumours. Hypoxia occurs when there is an imbalance between the amount of oxygen that is consumed by the cells and the amount that is supplied to them. This situation arises even in a highly vascularized environment. The vascularization in tumours is abnormal, with occlusions and blind ends. This means the vessels cannot properly oxygenate the nearby cells. Hypoxia is significant because it triggers tumour aggressivity. It is also known to generate drug resistance and is correlated with a bad prognosis.

items.item_title: How does hypoxia contribute to tumour aggressiveness?
items.text: Hypoxia contributes to the aggressiveness of tumours through several mechanisms. First, it causes changes in gene expression. This is mainly done through the HIF (hypoxia-induced Factor) family of transcription factors. These factors promote the expression of angiogenic factors, growth factors, and enzymes that remodel the extracellular matrix (ECM). Second, hypoxia results in the local recruitment of endothelial progenitor cells and mesenchymal stem cells. These cells then contribute further to hypoxia by secreting VEGF. Third, hypoxia is linked to DNA damage. Cycles of hypoxia and reoxygenation can produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), leading to oxidative stress. At the same time, hypoxia causes a downregulation of DNA repair pathways, all of which contributes to genomic instability.

items.item_title: What is the relationship between hypoxia and metastasis?
items.text: Hypoxia can allow tumour cells to escape from the hypoxic environment. This escape occurs through two processes: neovascularization and metastasis. The hypoxic conditions also affect the metabolism in the inner parts of the solid tumour. Glycolytic pathways are activated, a change known as the cancer metabolic shift. This metabolic shift generates an acidic pH in the extracellular environment, while the pH inside the tumour cells remains neutral. This acidity has been shown to promote cell migration. It does this by causing degradation of the extracellular matrix (ECM). Pro-metastatic factors are also induced by hypoxia, such as LOX, which remodels the ECM, and CTGF, an extracellular matrix protein.

items.item_title: How does hypoxia cause resistance to cancer therapies?
items.text: Hypoxia can have a critical impact on the effectiveness of anticancer therapies. Hypoxic regions are linked to necrosis and quiescence, a state of rest. Drugs that target rapidly growing cells will not be able to target these quiescent cancer cells. This inner part of the tumour is resistant to radiotherapy and chemotherapy. For example, hypoxic zones are insensitive to drugs that target p53-mediated apoptosis. Drug delivery to these hypoxic parts is also impaired because of the abnormal blood vessels. Finally, hypoxia can cause the transcriptional upregulation of genes that confer drug resistance. Mimicking the real oxygen exposure of cancer cells in in vitro assays is therefore important for assessing drug efficacy.

page_title: From 2D to 3D cell cultures

items.item_title: What are the limitations of two-dimensional cell systems?
items.text: A main issue with 2D cell systems is their inability to represent the specific architecture of a tissue. This structure is what determines the function of a tissue or an organ. Data generated from 2D cell culture systems is difficult to extrapolate to physiology. This is because these 2D cultures may only reflect a very small part of an organ's structure. Three-dimensional cell culture systems are considered to be closer to the complexity found in a living system. Because physiology is very complex, models must be continually refined to approach it. As experimental systems become more involved, many variables must be considered. It is necessary to cross information from mathematics, biological knowledge, and experimentation to determine the best parameters to study.

items.item_title: What are the main types of cells used for 3D cell culture?
items.text: There are three main categories of cells used for 3D cell culture. A balance must be found between availability, ease of use, and relevance. Primary human cells are one option. These are derived from a biopsy and can provide a pure population or a multicellular structure. Their main restriction is availability, as they are rare and difficult to obtain. Human stem cells or progenitor cells are another choice. These cells are more stable. With the discovery of IPS cells, their availability and ease of use are now compatible with routine 3D culture. The only drawback is related to their differentiation, which must be strictly followed to avoid non-desired cell types. Finally, cancer cell lines, which are normally strong cells, can be used. It is possible to build a 3D structure with them, but there is a risk of genetic changes modifying the results.

items.item_title: How is nutrient and mass transport different in 3D cultures compared to 2D?
items.text: A main requirement for 3D cell cultures is keeping the cellular environment controlled and standardised. It is important to provide sufficient nutrients and O2 to all cells. The waste products made by the cells must also be removed. In 2D cell culture systems, nutrients are provided in a homogeneous way. This is different in 3D cultures, where a gradient will appear. This gradient is due to the three-dimensional organization of the cells. The inner cells have less contact with nutrients and O2. The external layer of the culture is directly in contact with the media. If the growth rate is not properly controlled, it can lead to necrosis in a part of the cell culture. This is caused by a lack of oxygen and nutrients.

items.item_title: What is the importance of cell density in 3D cell cultures?
items.text: A main restriction in 3D cell cultures is related to the density of the culture. If the cell culture becomes overpopulated, a problem will arise. A group of cells within the structure will not receive enough nutrients and oxygen. As a result, these cells will die. This highlights the need to manage cell growth and density. The optimal density for a culture is not a single value. It depends on the kind of cells being used for the experiment.
